exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back.
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at your thighs.

Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back. Retract your shoulder blades and keep them contacting the bench.
Start with the dumbbells at mid-chest, elbows bent about 90 degrees and wrists neutral (dumbbell handles aligned with forearms).
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly straight but not hyperextended; keep wrists stacked over elbows.

Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position, maintaining control and stopping when the dumbbells reach chest level or your elbows form ~90 degrees. Keep elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from your torso to protect the shoulders.
Keep your core braced and feet firmly planted for stability throughout each rep; avoid excessive flaring of the ribs or letting the shoulders lift off the bench.
Use a spotter for heavy loads and choose a weight that allows controlled full-range reps with good form.
exercises.description: Flat dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a bench, pressing two dumbbells upward from chest level to develop the pectorals; the movement also recruits the anterior delts and triceps for stability and lockout.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.

exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and extend the arms straight above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement (do not lock the elbows).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, maintaining the slight elbow bend until you feel a stretch in the chest or your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep the shoulder blades stable on the bench (no excessive shrugging) and avoid letting the dumbbells travel behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then exhale and bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc, focusing on squeezing the pectorals until the weights meet above the sternum.
Perform each rep with controlled tempo and avoid bouncing or using momentum; select a weight that allows full control and a safe range of motion.
exercises.description: A supine resistance exercise performed with dumbbells that isolates the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from above the sternum to the sides, producing a deep stretch and concentric squeeze of the pectorals.

exercises.name: Cable Standing-Up Straight Crossovers
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to a high position (around or slightly above shoulder height) and attach single-hand handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to a high position (around or slightly above shoulder height) and attach single-hand handles.
Stand in the center of the cable station with feet hip-width apart (or a slight staggered stance for balance), torso upright and core braced.
Grasp a handle in each hand with a neutral grip, arms out to the sides at about shoulder level and a slight bend in the elbows; retract the shoulder blades slightly to start.

Exhale and pull both handles down and together in a smooth arc across the body until the hands meet or nearly meet in front of the sternum, keeping the elbows slightly bent and avoiding excessive torso lean or rotation.
Pause briefly and squeeze the pectorals at the end range, then inhale and slowly resist the cables as you return your arms to the starting position under control.
Maintain an upright posture, keep the core engaged, avoid shrugging the shoulders, and use a full controlled range of motion rather than momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable crossovers are a bilateral cable fly variation performed upright that brings the hands together in front of the body to load and concentrically shorten the chest (pectorals). The movement uses a fixed slight elbow bend and controlled return to emphasize chest contraction while the core stabilizes the torso.

exercises.name: Cable Middle Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to about mid-chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to about mid-chest height and select an appropriate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered stance (one foot slightly forward) with a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip and extend your arms out to the sides so there is tension on the cables; keep a small, constant bend in the elbows.
Retract your shoulder blades and keep the shoulders down; brace your core to prevent torso movement.
Exhale and bring the handles together in front of your chest in a wide arc, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Inhale and slowly reverse the motion with control, allowing the arms to return to the starting position until you feel a gentle stretch in the chest.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding torso leaning, shoulder shrugging, or locking the elbows.
exercises.description: Standing bilateral cable fly performed with the pulleys set at mid-chest height; the exercise horizontally adducts the arms in a controlled arc to load and contract the pectorals. A staggered stance and slight elbow bend are used to stabilize and protect the joints while the chest performs the work.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered or shoulder-width stance with knees soft and core braced (slight forward lean at the hips).
Reach down and grab each handle with a neutral or slightly supinated grip, palms facing in or up, elbows slightly bent and fixed in that position throughout the movement.
Begin with the arms out to the sides and low (handles near hips or knees) and inhale.
Exhale and pull the handles upward and inward in a wide arc, keeping the elbows slightly bent, bringing the hands together or in front of the chest/upper sternum.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze the pectorals while avoiding excessive shoulder shrugging.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, allowing the cables to return the arms to the start position while maintaining tension in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions and re-rack safely; keep torso stable and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly is a low-to-high cable chest fly that targets the pectorals by sweeping the arms upward and together from low pulley positions; performed standing with a slight forward lean and a soft bend in the elbows to emphasize the chest contraction.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.

Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Lever Incline Chest Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine backrest so the torso is supported at an incline (about 30–45 degrees) and set an appropriate load on the weight pegs.
instructions: Adjust the machine backrest so the torso is supported at an incline (about 30–45 degrees) and set an appropriate load on the weight pegs.
Sit with your back fully against the pad, feet flat on the floor or foot platform, and hips stable; grasp the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip at chest/upper‑chest level.
Before initiating the rep, draw your shoulder blades slightly down and back to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a tight chest.
Exhale and press the handles upward and slightly forward in a controlled motion, extending the elbows but avoiding aggressive locking at the top.
Pause briefly at the top with the arms nearly extended, keeping tension in the pectorals and delts.
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back down to the starting position so the handles approach the upper‑chest area, maintaining control through the eccentric phase.
Keep the lower back and buttocks in contact with the pad throughout the set; avoid excessive arching or thrusting of the hips.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions with a smooth tempo; reduce weight if you cannot control the lowering or maintain form.
exercises.description: Seated incline lever chest press performed on a plate-loaded or selectorized lever machine that targets the upper portion of the pectorals by pressing handles upward and slightly forward from an inclined, back-supported position. It emphasizes controlled concentric pressing and slow eccentric lowering for upper‑chest development.

exercises.name: Barbell Feet Flat Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
instructions: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
Lie supine on the flat bench with your eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and planted for stability, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades (pinch them together and press them into the bench) to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the bar with a full grip slightly wider than shoulder width, wrists stacked over forearms and elbows under the bar.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar into the start position directly above your shoulders.
Inhale, then lower the bar in a controlled manner to mid-chest / nipple line, keeping elbows at about a 45° angle to the torso and maintaining scapular retraction.
Briefly pause with the bar lightly contacting the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar upward along a slightly arced path back to full arm extension.
Lock out the elbows at the top without overextending, keep the feet planted and core braced throughout the set.
After the final rep, carefully rack the bar onto the supports or have your spotter assist; use a spotter for heavy loads and maintain strict control on every rep.
exercises.description: Barbell feet-flat flat bench press is a supine pressing exercise that primarily works the pectorals by pressing a loaded barbell from the chest to full arm extension. Keeping the feet flat provides a stable base while the triceps and delts assist in elbow extension and shoulder stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench to a comfortable angle and hook your feet under the foot pads so your body is stable; lie supine with your head lower than your hips.
instructions: Set a decline bench to a comfortable angle and hook your feet under the foot pads so your body is stable; lie supine with your head lower than your hips.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand and hold them above your chest with a pronated (palms facing forward) grip and arms extended but not locked.
Retract your shoulder blades slightly and brace your core to maintain a stable torso throughout the movement.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled arc to the lower chest/ribcage area, keeping your elbows at about a 30–45° angle from your torso.
Pause briefly when the dumbbells reach chest level (do not bounce); maintain control and a steady posture with feet anchored.
Exhale and press the dumbbells upward until your arms are nearly straight without locking the elbows, squeezing the pectorals at the top.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then carefully return the dumbbells to your thighs and sit up to place them down to exit the bench safely.
Coaching cues: avoid excessive lower back arch, keep movements controlled, and do not let the elbows flare directly out at 90° to reduce shoulder strain.
exercises.description: Lying on a decline bench with feet secured, press a pair of dumbbells upward to emphasize the lower portion of the pectoral muscles. The movement is a pressing exercise performed with a pronated grip and controlled lowering to the chest.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench in a rack and load the barbell with an appropriate weight; place your feet securely under the decline foot pads.
instructions: Set a decline bench in a rack and load the barbell with an appropriate weight; place your feet securely under the decline foot pads.
Sit on the bench, then lie back so your head is lower than your hips (decline position) and hook your feet under the pads to prevent sliding.
Grip the bar with a slightly wider-than-shoulder-width overhand grip, wrists stacked over elbows and shoulder blades retracted and depressed.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar over your lower chest/upper sternum; have a spotter if lifting heavy.
Inhale, then lower the bar in a controlled manner to touch or come just above the lower chest/upper abdomen (lower sternum), keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle to the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, then exhale and press the bar upward by extending the elbows until the arms are nearly straight (do not lock aggressively).
Maintain tightness through the chest and core, keep the scapulae retracted, and avoid excessive arching of the lower back throughout the set.
Control the descent on each rep and re-rack the bar safely with the help of a spotter when finished.
exercises.description: A barbell pressing movement performed on a decline bench that emphasizes the lower portion of the pectorals. Lying with the head lower than the hips, you lower the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen and press it back up.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles or place your forearms against the pads with a slight bend in the elbows; start with arms out to the sides and shoulder blades pulled back and down.
Exhale and drive the handles forward and together in a smooth arc by contracting the pectorals, keeping the elbows slightly bent and wrists neutral.
Pause briefly when the hands/handles meet or are directly in front of your chest, feeling the squeeze in the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly allow the levers to return to the start position under control, maintaining shoulder blade stability and avoiding rapid letting go or trunk leaning.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; use a weight that allows full control of both concentric and eccentric phases without excessive shoulder or back movement.
exercises.description: Seated machine chest fly performed on a lever/pec-deck: you bring the arms together in front of the chest against resistance to isolate the pectoral muscles. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and controlled arc to emphasize the inner chest.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench between two cable towers (about 30–45° incline).
instructions: Set an incline bench between two cable towers (about 30–45° incline). Attach single D‑handles and set the pulleys to low or just below chest height so the cables travel upward across your chest.
Sit on the bench and lie back so your head rests on the pad; grip one handle in each hand with a neutral or palms‑facing posture and feet flat on the floor.
Retract your shoulder blades and brace your core so your back stays pressed into the bench (inclined supine position). Keep a slight, fixed bend in your elbows throughout the exercise.
Start with your arms out to the sides in line with your chest (open position) and inhale, then exhale as you bring the handles together in a wide arc toward the centerline of your upper chest.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction, focusing on squeezing the pectorals and keeping the elbows slightly bent and stable (avoid driving motion from the wrists or excessive shoulder elevation).
Slowly reverse the movement on the inhale, controlling the cables as you return to the start position until you feel a stretch in the chest without losing scapular retraction.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining core engagement and avoiding excessive momentum or arching of the lower back.
exercises.description: Lying on an incline bench placed between two cable towers, you perform a bilateral fly motion with a slight elbow bend to concentrically bring the handles together and target the upper chest. The movement emphasizes a horizontal adduction arc while the core stabilizes the torso.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

warmup.notes: Brief warmup to activate target muscles. Can skip if first working set feels warm.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
instructions: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
Lie supine on the incline bench with your head supported, feet flat on the floor, and back slightly arched while maintaining scapular retraction.
Grip the barbell with a pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your upper chest/shoulder line, then stabilize before starting the descent.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the upper chest/clavicular area, keeping elbows at roughly a 30–60° angle from the torso (avoid flaring to 90°).
Lightly touch or stop just above the chest, pause briefly without bouncing, then exhale and press the bar upward and slightly back to the start position, fully extending the elbows.
Keep core braced and feet planted throughout to maintain stability and prevent excessive lower back extension.
Carefully re-rack the bar under control or have a spotter assist when finished.
exercises.description: A barbell press performed on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest by pressing the bar upward and slightly toward the clavicle. It also engages the front delts and triceps for pressing and locking out the weight.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Low Fly
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and a slight hinge at the hips (standing, slight forward lean).
instructions: Start standing with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and a slight hinge at the hips (standing, slight forward lean).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip at or just above thigh level (low position). Keep the spine neutral and shoulders down.
Maintain a small, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement; do not lock the joints.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells in a wide arc up and inward, leading with the elbows so the hands meet or come close in front of the upper chest/shoulder level (low-to-high arc).
Pause briefly at the top and actively squeeze the pectorals while keeping the shoulders stable (avoid shrugging).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back along the same arc to the starting low position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding momentum or excessive back extension.
exercises.description: A standing low-to-high dumbbell fly that moves the weights in an upward arc from near the hips to in front of the chest, emphasizing the pectorals. Keep a slight bend in the elbows and squeeze the chest at the top of the movement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Single Arm Cross Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in one hand with a light, controlled grip (working arm).
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in one hand with a light, controlled grip (working arm).
Begin with the working arm down and slightly away from the body, elbow soft (slightly bent) and palm facing inward or slightly up.
Brace your core and keep the torso stable — avoid twisting or leaning toward the working arm throughout the movement.
Raise the dumbbell across your body in a horizontal arc (shoulder horizontal adduction) toward the opposite shoulder or mid-chest level, leading with the elbow and keeping the bend constant.
At the top of the movement, pause briefly and squeeze the pectoral muscles, feeling the contraction in the chest.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the start position under control, resisting momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Use a lighter weight if you find the shoulder compensating or the torso leaning.
exercises.description: A unilateral standing cable/dumbbell fly variation where you move the arm across the body in a horizontal adduction arc to emphasize the chest. Performed standing with a slight elbow bend to isolate the pectoral fibers while the anterior deltoid assists.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back.
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at your thighs.

Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back. Retract your shoulder blades and keep them contacting the bench.
Start with the dumbbells at mid-chest, elbows bent about 90 degrees and wrists neutral (dumbbell handles aligned with forearms).
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly straight but not hyperextended; keep wrists stacked over elbows.

Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position, maintaining control and stopping when the dumbbells reach chest level or your elbows form ~90 degrees. Keep elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from your torso to protect the shoulders.
Keep your core braced and feet firmly planted for stability throughout each rep; avoid excessive flaring of the ribs or letting the shoulders lift off the bench.
Use a spotter for heavy loads and choose a weight that allows controlled full-range reps with good form.
exercises.description: Flat dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a bench, pressing two dumbbells upward from chest level to develop the pectorals; the movement also recruits the anterior delts and triceps for stability and lockout.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline One Arm Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a dumbbell of appropriate weight beside the bench.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a dumbbell of appropriate weight beside the bench.
Sit on the bench and pick up the dumbbell with one hand using a neutral grip; plant both feet flat on the floor for stability.
Lie back so your upper back and head are supported; retract your working-side scapula slightly and keep your chest lifted.
Position the dumbbell at shoulder level with your elbow bent (~90 degrees) and your forearm vertical or slightly angled; use your free hand on your thigh or bench for extra balance if needed.
Brace your core and press the dumbbell upward and slightly inward until your arm is almost fully extended (avoid locking the elbow), exhaling as you push.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining control and shoulder stability, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position under control.
Keep your torso stable and avoid rotating toward the working side; complete the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat.
exercises.description: A single-arm dumbbell press performed on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest while requiring shoulder and triceps stabilization. Performed one arm at a time to challenge unilateral strength and core stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Low Fly
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and a slight hinge at the hips (standing, slight forward lean).
instructions: Start standing with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and a slight hinge at the hips (standing, slight forward lean).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip at or just above thigh level (low position). Keep the spine neutral and shoulders down.
Maintain a small, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement; do not lock the joints.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells in a wide arc up and inward, leading with the elbows so the hands meet or come close in front of the upper chest/shoulder level (low-to-high arc).
Pause briefly at the top and actively squeeze the pectorals while keeping the shoulders stable (avoid shrugging).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back along the same arc to the starting low position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding momentum or excessive back extension.
exercises.description: A standing low-to-high dumbbell fly that moves the weights in an upward arc from near the hips to in front of the chest, emphasizing the pectorals. Keep a slight bend in the elbows and squeeze the chest at the top of the movement.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.

Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise
exercises.notes: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
instructions: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
Attach wrist straps to low pulley handles (both sides) and set a light–moderate weight so you can control the entire range.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the bench, and grasp the wrist straps so your arms hang down in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows; palms should face each other.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Clean and Press
exercises.notes: Step 1: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a full overhand or mixed grip and keep the chest up and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Step 1: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a full overhand or mixed grip and keep the chest up and core braced (standing position).
Step 2: Initiate the lift with a hip hinge by bending at the hips and knees keeping the spine neutral and shoulders slightly in front of the bar; maintain a tight core and engaged lats.

Step 3: Drive through the heels and extend the hips, knees and ankles explosively (triple extension) to accelerate the bar upward while shrugging the shoulders and pulling the elbows high and out (clean pull).

Step 4: As the bar reaches peak upward momentum, quickly drop under the bar into a partial front squat and rotate the elbows forward to receive the bar on the front of the shoulders in a front-rack position (clean, front-rack).

Step 5: Stand tall from the front-rack by extending the hips and knees to full standing, reset your core and grip, then press the bar overhead by extending the elbows and driving the head slightly forward until arms are fully locked out (press).
Step 6: Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position, then carefully return the bar to the floor with a hip hinge and bend the knees to reset for the next repetition.

Step 7: Maintain braced abs and a neutral spine throughout, breathe consistently (exhale during the press), and use controlled tempo—perform the clean explosively but receive and press with stability and control.
exercises.description: A compound Olympic-style lift combining a barbell clean (lifting the bar to a front-rack position) followed immediately by a press overhead, training the shoulders along with legs, hips and upper back. It develops explosive power, pressing strength, and full-body coordination.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Front Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back firmly supported and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back firmly supported and feet flat on the floor.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or overhand grip and let your arms hang straight down at your sides, wrists neutral and a slight bend in the elbows.

Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and exhale as you raise both dumbbells together in front of you in a controlled arc to about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Keep the elbows slightly bent and wrists firm; avoid swinging or using momentum from the torso or legs.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, choosing a weight that allows strict form; stop if you feel shoulder joint pain.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, both dumbbells are raised straight forward to shoulder height to emphasize the anterior portion of the delts. The incline and controlled motion reduce momentum and isolate the front shoulder muscles.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Cable Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set the pulley to the highest position and attach the pro-lat bar; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set the pulley to the highest position and attach the pro-lat bar; select an appropriate weight.
Sit on the bench with knees secured under the pads and feet flat on the floor (seated position).
Reach up and grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Sit tall with a neutral spine and chest slightly lifted; lean back only a few degrees (small recline) — do not arch the lower back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows down and back, pulling the bar toward the upper chest/clavicle while leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Hold and squeeze the lats briefly at the bottom of the movement without leaning excessively backward.
Slowly and under control allow the bar to return to the start position until the arms are nearly fully extended and the shoulder blades re-expand.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining controlled tempo and avoiding momentum or swinging.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a pro-lat bar targets the latissimus dorsi with a wide overhand grip, pulling the bar down toward the upper chest. The movement also engages the biceps, rear delts and upper back muscles to stabilize and complete the pull.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent Over Scapula Row
exercises.notes: Set up with one knee and the same-side hand resting on a flat bench (kneeling on bench), the opposite foot planted on the floor for stability; grip a dumbbell in the free hand with the arm hanging straight toward the floor.
instructions: Set up with one knee and the same-side hand resting on a flat bench (kneeling on bench), the opposite foot planted on the floor for stability; grip a dumbbell in the free hand with the arm hanging straight toward the floor.
Hinge forward at the hips so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor while keeping a neutral spine and bracing your core; maintain a slight bend in the supporting knee.

Begin the movement by retracting and depressing the scapula of the working side (pull the shoulder blade back and down) while keeping the elbow nearly straight or allowing only a small bend; do not initiate the movement by bending the elbow.
Squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1 second, feeling the contraction across the upper back and between the shoulder blades.
Slowly allow the scapula to protract and return the arm to the fully extended starting position in a controlled manner.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides so the previously supporting hand/knee becomes the working side. Maintain neutral spine and avoid torso rotation throughout.
exercises.description: A bent-over, bench-supported dumbbell row that emphasizes scapular retraction to target the upper back and mid-trapezius/rhomboid area. Performed from a kneeling/bent-over position with a single-arm dumbbell, focusing on pulling the shoulder blade back and down.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Grip Incline Bench Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
Load a barbell and grasp it with a shoulder-width reverse (underhand) grip so palms face up and arms hang straight down toward the floor.
Start with your chest pressed into the bench, shoulders relaxed and scapulae slightly protracted, core engaged and neck neutral.

Initiate the movement by retracting your scapulae and pulling the barbell upward in a controlled path toward your lower chest / upper abdomen, leading with the elbows and keeping them close to your sides.
Pause 1 second at the top and squeeze the lats and upper back without hyperextending the spine or lifting your chest off the bench.
Lower the barbell slowly back to the start with full arm extension while maintaining tension in the lats; avoid dropping the weight or using momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out while pulling and inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: Prone incline bench row performed with a reverse (underhand) grip that emphasizes the lats while also engaging the upper back and arms. Lying chest-down on an incline bench, you pull a barbell up toward the lower chest/upper abdomen and lower it under control.

exercises.name: Assisted Pull-up
exercises.notes: Set the assistance weight on the machine so you can complete the desired reps with good form.
instructions: Set the assistance weight on the machine so you can complete the desired reps with good form.
Kneel on the machine's knee pad (or place knees on the assistance platform) and reach up to grasp the pull-up bar with a shoulder-width, pronated (overhand) grip; keep head neutral.
Before pulling, pull your shoulder blades down and back (scapular retraction) to engage the lats and upper back.
Pull your chest upward toward the bar by driving the elbows down and back, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging.
Pause briefly at the top with your chin over the bar, maintaining scapular retraction.
Slowly lower yourself back to the starting position until your arms are fully extended, maintaining control throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions, and adjust assistance if reps become too easy or too difficult.
exercises.description: Kneeling on an assisted pull-up machine, the exerciser pulls their torso up toward an overhead bar using a counterweight to reduce load; this movement primarily trains the latissimus dorsi and upper back pulling muscles. Control the descent to emphasize the eccentric phase.

exercises.name: Lever Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Load the plate-loaded lever machine to the desired weight and stand facing forward with feet hip-width apart, toes pointing straight ahead.
instructions: Load the plate-loaded lever machine to the desired weight and stand facing forward with feet hip-width apart, toes pointing straight ahead.
Grasp the machine handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; maintain a neutral spine and soft knees.
Keep your torso upright and immobile; do not lean or use leg drive throughout the movement.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears by driving the scapulae upward—think of trying to touch your shoulders to your ears.
Hold the top position and squeeze the traps and levator scapulae for 1–2 seconds while keeping the arms straight and grip firm (forearms engaged).
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the starting position under control until the traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the prescribed repetitions; avoid rolling or rotating the shoulders and avoid excessive neck extension—keep your head neutral and eyes forward.
exercises.description: Standing plate-loaded lever shrugs target the upper trapezius by elevating the shoulders while holding the machine handles; the lift is a vertical scapular elevation with a controlled lowering phase.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
