exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

exercises.name: Lever Chest Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles sit roughly at mid-chest level when you grasp them; select an appropriate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles sit roughly at mid-chest level when you grasp them; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your back and head firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, wrists aligned with forearms and shoulders relaxed away from ears.
Begin with elbows slightly below shoulder height and forearms pointed forward (starting position).
Exhale and press the handles forward and slightly together by extending your arms until nearly straight; avoid locking the elbows at full extension.
Pause briefly at the end of the press while maintaining control and keeping the chest engaged.
Inhale and slowly return the handles back to the starting position, controlling the weight and stopping when elbows reach roughly the same angle as the start.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping the torso stable and avoiding arching the lower back throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated lever chest press performed on a machine to push handles forward using the chest muscles; the movement also involves the front delts and triceps. Use a controlled pressing motion from mid-chest to near full arm extension without locking the elbows.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench to a comfortable angle and hook your feet under the foot pads so your body is stable; lie supine with your head lower than your hips. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set a decline bench to a comfortable angle and hook your feet under the foot pads so your body is stable; lie supine with your head lower than your hips.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand and hold them above your chest with a pronated (palms facing forward) grip and arms extended but not locked.
Retract your shoulder blades slightly and brace your core to maintain a stable torso throughout the movement.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled arc to the lower chest/ribcage area, keeping your elbows at about a 30–45° angle from your torso.
Pause briefly when the dumbbells reach chest level (do not bounce); maintain control and a steady posture with feet anchored.
Exhale and press the dumbbells upward until your arms are nearly straight without locking the elbows, squeezing the pectorals at the top.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then carefully return the dumbbells to your thighs and sit up to place them down to exit the bench safely.
Coaching cues: avoid excessive lower back arch, keep movements controlled, and do not let the elbows flare directly out at 90° to reduce shoulder strain.
exercises.description: Lying on a decline bench with feet secured, press a pair of dumbbells upward to emphasize the lower portion of the pectoral muscles. The movement is a pressing exercise performed with a pronated grip and controlled lowering to the chest.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Single Arm Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat (and chest pad if available) so the handle is reachable with your arm fully extended; select an appropriate weight and place feet flat on the foot platform. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat (and chest pad if available) so the handle is reachable with your arm fully extended; select an appropriate weight and place feet flat on the foot platform.
Sit upright with a neutral spine, chest lightly against the pad or upright on the seat, feet planted, and grasp the single handle with a neutral or overhand grip.
Begin with the working arm fully extended and the shoulder relaxed; brace your core and prevent torso rotation.
Exhale and pull the handle by driving the elbow straight back toward your hip, leading with the elbow and actively retracting the shoulder blade.
Pause 1–2 seconds at the top and squeeze the lats and upper back while keeping the torso still.
Inhale and slowly control the handle back to the start, fully extending the arm without collapsing the shoulder.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides; maintain a controlled tempo and avoid using momentum or twisting the torso.
exercises.description: A unilateral seated row performed on a lever machine that emphasizes the lats by pulling a single handle back while keeping the torso stable. Each rep focuses on driving the elbow toward the hip and retracting the shoulder blade for a strong concentric contraction.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.

exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent ~90°, palms facing forward or slightly inward and wrists neutral.
Inhale, keep the spine neutral and shoulders down away from the ears.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled motion until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead (avoid locking the elbows).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and keeping the torso stable.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height with control to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Use a lighter weight or a spotter if form breaks down or if pressing heavy loads.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press is a compound pressing movement performed from a seated, back‑supported position that primarily develops the delts while also engaging the triceps and upper back for stabilization.

exercises.name: Lever Chest Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles sit roughly at mid-chest level when you grasp them; select an appropriate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles sit roughly at mid-chest level when you grasp them; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your back and head firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, wrists aligned with forearms and shoulders relaxed away from ears.
Begin with elbows slightly below shoulder height and forearms pointed forward (starting position).
Exhale and press the handles forward and slightly together by extending your arms until nearly straight; avoid locking the elbows at full extension.
Pause briefly at the end of the press while maintaining control and keeping the chest engaged.
Inhale and slowly return the handles back to the starting position, controlling the weight and stopping when elbows reach roughly the same angle as the start.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping the torso stable and avoiding arching the lower back throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated lever chest press performed on a machine to push handles forward using the chest muscles; the movement also involves the front delts and triceps. Use a controlled pressing motion from mid-chest to near full arm extension without locking the elbows.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with your arms fully extended; keep your head aligned with your spine.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows back and up, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs or hip area while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, maintaining tightness through the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with controlled motion until your arms are extended.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, keeping the torso fixed (avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation) and continue to breathe: exhale while pulling, inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A bent-over row performed with dumbbells where you hinge at the hips and pull the weights toward your torso, emphasizing the lats and upper back. The movement also engages the arms and posterior chain to stabilize the hinged position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Decline Fly
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet in the foot pads so you can remain stable while supine. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set a decline bench to a moderate angle and secure your feet in the foot pads so you can remain stable while supine.
Pick two light-to-moderate dumbbells and sit down; then lie back so your head is lower than your hips (decline position) and your feet are anchored.
Start with the dumbbells held directly above your lower chest, palms facing each other (neutral grip), arms almost straight but with a slight, fixed bend in the elbows.
Brace your core and keep your shoulder blades pulled slightly together to stabilize the shoulder girdle.

Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to your sides in a controlled wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend; aim to lower until you feel a stretch in the lower chest but do not let the elbows drop behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, maintaining tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior.
Exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc using your chest, bringing them to meet above your lower sternum; avoid using momentum or locking the elbows at the top.
Perform each rep with a controlled tempo (2–4 seconds descent, 1 second concentric) and keep the wrists neutral; reduce weight if you feel shoulder pain.
When finished, bring the dumbbells safely to your thighs and sit up slowly to avoid dizziness from the decline position.
exercises.description: Lying supine on a decline bench, the dumbbell decline fly isolates the lower portion of the pectorals by moving the arms in a wide arc with a fixed elbow angle. It stretches the chest on the descent and contracts the chest to bring the weights together over the sternum.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Pulldown (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and thigh pads so your feet are flat on the floor and your thighs are secured under the pads; sit upright with your chest against the chest pad if present. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and thigh pads so your feet are flat on the floor and your thighs are secured under the pads; sit upright with your chest against the chest pad if present.
Load the desired plates on the machine and grasp the overhead lever/bar with an overhand (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Start with your arms fully extended overhead, shoulders relaxed, and a slight natural arch in your spine — do not excessively lean back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows down and back, pulling the lever toward your upper chest; lead with the elbows rather than the hands.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades as you reach the bottom of the motion and pause briefly while squeezing the lats.
Slowly return the lever to the start position under control until your arms are fully extended and the shoulders are not rounded forward.
Keep the torso still and avoid using momentum or excessive backward lean; breathe out while pulling down and inhale while returning to the start.
exercises.description: A seated, plate-loaded pulldown performed on a lever machine that targets the latissimus dorsi by pulling a fixed lever down from overhead toward the upper chest while keeping the torso stable. Emphasize elbow-driven movement and scapular retraction to maximize lat engagement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.

Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.

exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.

exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing inward), arms fully relaxed.
Brace your core and press your lower back lightly into the bench to maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Slightly bend your elbows (soft arms) and keep the bend constant for the entire movement.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a controlled arc until your arms are roughly parallel to the floor (shoulder height); lead with the elbows and keep wrists neutral.
Keep shoulders down (do not shrug) and avoid leaning or using torso momentum—if you need to swing, reduce the weight.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position.
Perform repetitions with a steady tempo (e.g., 1–2 seconds up, 1–2 seconds down) and choose a weight that allows strict form.
exercises.description: A seated lateral raise isolates the lateral head of the deltoid by lifting light-to-moderate dumbbells out to the sides from a seated, supported position. Use a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to emphasize shoulder abduction and limit momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Single Arm Front Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent at ~90°, and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent at ~90°, and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip (palm facing your thigh or down). Place the non-working hand on the opposite thigh for support.
Start with the working arm hanging down in front of your hip, elbow slightly bent and shoulder relaxed.

Exhale and raise the dumbbell straight forward in a controlled arc until your arm is parallel to the floor (about shoulder height). Keep a slight bend in the elbow and lead with the hand, not the elbow.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the torso still — avoid leaning back or using hip drive.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position under control.
Perform the desired reps on one side, then switch arms. Maintain steady tempo and avoid swinging or using momentum.
exercises.description: A seated single-arm front raise isolates the front (anterior) deltoid by lifting a dumbbell straight in front of the body to shoulder height while seated, minimizing momentum. It strengthens shoulder flexion and improves anterior shoulder definition and stability.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Alternate Vertical Front Raises
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet shoulder‑width apart and a slight bend in the knees, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (neutral grip). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand upright with feet shoulder‑width apart and a slight bend in the knees, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (neutral grip).
Keep your core braced (abs engaged) and shoulders down and back to avoid upper back rounding or low‑back arching.
Begin the movement by slightly bending the elbow of the working arm and lifting the dumbbell straight up in front of you in a vertical plane (thumb leading or neutral), keeping the wrist neutral.
Raise the weight in a controlled motion until the arm reaches shoulder level or just above (eye/ear height), avoiding momentum or body swing.
Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position under control.

Alternate arms, performing the same controlled lift on the opposite side while the other arm returns to the start; maintain an even tempo and steady breathing (exhale on the lift, inhale on the lower).
Do not use excessive weight that causes torso movement; if you arch your back or swing, reduce the load to preserve proper delt isolation.
exercises.description: A standing, single‑arm dumbbell front raise performed alternately to isolate the anterior deltoid while the opposite side rests. The exercise uses a controlled vertical lifting path to raise the weight in front of the body and slowly lower it back down.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to a near‑vertical angle (about 75–85°) and place the barbell on a rack at roughly upper‑chest/shoulder height so you can unrack safely while seated.
instructions: Set an incline bench to a near‑vertical angle (about 75–85°) and place the barbell on a rack at roughly upper‑chest/shoulder height so you can unrack safely while seated.
Sit with your back pressed fully against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core. Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder‑width with palms facing forward.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving it forward until it is positioned above your shoulders; keep scapulae lightly retracted and chest up.
Lower the bar in a controlled manner to the upper‑chest / clavicle area, keeping your elbows slightly in front of the torso (about 30–45°) to protect the shoulder joint.
Drive the bar upward explosively (but controlled), exhaling as you press, until your arms are nearly fully extended overhead without hyperextending the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top to maintain tension, then inhale and lower the bar slowly back to the starting upper‑chest position.
After completing the set, carefully re‑rack the bar by guiding it back onto the rack hooks while seated or have a spotter assist if using heavy loads.
exercises.description: Seated incline barbell shoulder press performed on a near‑vertical bench; presses a barbell upward from the upper‑chest/shoulder level to train the delts with assistance from the triceps and upper chest. Back is supported by the incline bench to isolate the shoulders.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Clean and Press
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet about hip-width to slightly wider, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a double overhand or hook grip and chest up.
instructions: Start standing with feet about hip-width to slightly wider, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a double overhand or hook grip and chest up.
Hinge at the hips and bend the knees so your hips are low, back flat, shoulders slightly in front of the bar and eyes forward (front of a squat rack/sightline).
Initiate the lift by driving through the heels and extending the hips and knees explosively while keeping the bar close to your body; shrug forcefully at full extension.

As you shrug, pull the elbows high and outside to accelerate the bar upward and quickly drop under the bar into a partial front-rack squat to 'catch' the bar on your shoulders (front rack position) with elbows high.
Stand up tall from the catch, fully extending the hips and knees to a stable upright front-rack position with the bar resting on the shoulders/clavicles and elbows forward.

From the front-rack, press the bar overhead by driving through the legs slightly (if using a push press) or strictly extend the arms (strict press), finishing with arms locked overhead and head slightly forward under the bar.

Lower the bar back to the front-rack in a controlled manner, then return the bar to the floor by hinging at the hips and bending the knees (reset) or carefully drop into a controlled descent if using appropriate technique and environment.
Maintain a braced core and neutral spine throughout, keep the bar path close to the body, and breathe/exhale during the press portion; reset between reps if necessary to preserve form.
exercises.description: A compound Olympic-style lift that combines a barbell clean (explosive hip extension and pull to bring the bar to the front rack) with a strict or push overhead press to finish. Trains shoulder pressing strength while recruiting legs, posterior chain, upper back and core for power and stability.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Twist Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with feet planted flat on the floor.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with feet planted flat on the floor.
Pick up a pair of dumbbells and lie supine on the incline bench; hold the dumbbells at shoulder level with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and elbows bent about 45° from your torso.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly so your upper back stays in contact with the bench.
Exhale and press both dumbbells up and slightly together while rotating your wrists so that at the top your palms face forward and the dumbbells are parallel or lightly touching.
Pause briefly at the top and consciously squeeze the upper chest (pectorals).
Inhale and lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner back to the start position while rotating the wrists back to the neutral grip, keeping elbows under the wrists.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a controlled tempo, full scapular stability, and avoiding flaring the elbows excessively outward.
exercises.description: An incline dumbbell press with a twisting motion that emphasizes the upper chest while adding a rotational component to engage the shoulders and forearms. Performed lying supine on an incline bench, pressing the weights up and rotating the wrists so the palms face forward at the top of the movement.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Single Arm Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set a single-handle attachment to a high pulley and place an incline bench facing the machine so your chest will be supported when leaned forward (bench angle ~30–45°).
instructions: Set a single-handle attachment to a high pulley and place an incline bench facing the machine so your chest will be supported when leaned forward (bench angle ~30–45°).
Sit on the bench and position yourself prone/leaning forward with your chest against the bench pad, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Reach up with one hand and grab the handle with a neutral or overhand grip; allow the arm to be fully extended overhead and relax the shoulder.
Before pulling, draw your working scapula down and back (scapular retraction/depression) to initiate the lats and avoid shoulder shrugging.
Pull the handle down and back in a smooth arc, driving your elbow toward your hip while keeping the elbow close to the torso; focus on contracting the lats.
Hold briefly at the bottom with a strong lat squeeze, keeping your chest pressed to the bench and avoiding torso rotation.
Slowly return the handle to the start position under control until the arm is nearly extended, maintaining scapular control.
Perform the prescribed reps, then switch sides. Breathe out while pulling down and breathe in while returning to the start.
exercises.description: Performed from an incline bench facing a high cable, this single-arm pulldown emphasizes a unilateral lat contraction while the chest is supported. The movement pulls the handle down and back toward the hip to load the lats and upper back.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.

The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise
exercises.notes: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
instructions: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
Attach wrist straps to low pulley handles (both sides) and set a light–moderate weight so you can control the entire range.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the bench, and grasp the wrist straps so your arms hang down in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows; palms should face each other.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

workout.title: Upper Body - STRENGTH
workout.description: Upper Body workout focusing on strength training.

exercises.name: Dumbbell One Arm Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft; hold a dumbbell in one hand at shoulder height with elbow bent and the palm facing forward or neutral.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft; hold a dumbbell in one hand at shoulder height with elbow bent and the palm facing forward or neutral. Place the free hand on your hip to help limit torso rotation.
Brace your core and gently squeeze your glutes to prevent low-back arching; keep the chest up and head neutral.
Exhale and press the dumbbell straight overhead by extending the arm, keeping the elbow under the wrist and the motion controlled.
Pause briefly at the top with the arm nearly straight (do not hyperextend the elbow) and keep the shoulder depressed (avoid shrugging up toward the ear).
Inhale and lower the dumbbell slowly and under control back to the starting position at shoulder height.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch sides. Reduce weight if you notice excessive torso lean, back arching, or loss of control.
exercises.description: A single-arm overhead dumbbell press performed standing to develop shoulder (delts) strength and stability while engaging the triceps and core for control. The opposite hand is often placed on the hip to limit torso rotation.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Grip Incline Bench Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
Load a barbell and grasp it with a shoulder-width reverse (underhand) grip so palms face up and arms hang straight down toward the floor.
Start with your chest pressed into the bench, shoulders relaxed and scapulae slightly protracted, core engaged and neck neutral.

Initiate the movement by retracting your scapulae and pulling the barbell upward in a controlled path toward your lower chest / upper abdomen, leading with the elbows and keeping them close to your sides.
Pause 1 second at the top and squeeze the lats and upper back without hyperextending the spine or lifting your chest off the bench.
Lower the barbell slowly back to the start with full arm extension while maintaining tension in the lats; avoid dropping the weight or using momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out while pulling and inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: Prone incline bench row performed with a reverse (underhand) grip that emphasizes the lats while also engaging the upper back and arms. Lying chest-down on an incline bench, you pull a barbell up toward the lower chest/upper abdomen and lower it under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Alternative Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand. Plant your feet flat on the floor and lie back so your head, upper back, and butt are supported (supine on incline bench).
Hold the dumbbells with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and press them together over your upper chest with a small bend in both elbows; this is the start position.
Keep your shoulder blades lightly retracted and braced throughout the set to protect the shoulder joint.

Lower one dumbbell in a slow, controlled arc out to the side (maintain the same elbow bend) until you feel a stretch in the upper chest; keep the opposite arm stationary and slightly elevated above the chest.
Pause briefly at the bottom for tension on the pectorals, then drive that dumbbell back up in the same arc to meet the elevated dumbbell above the chest, squeezing the pectorals at the top.
Alternate sides each repetition, maintaining control, a constant elbow angle, and avoiding bouncing or letting the lowered elbow drop behind the plane of your torso.
Breathe in as you lower the weight and exhale as you bring it back up. Use a weight that allows strict form and full control for the full range of motion.
Avoid shrugging the shoulders or overarching the lower back; stop immediately if you feel sharp shoulder pain and reduce range or load.
exercises.description: Lying supine on an incline bench, perform alternating single-arm dumbbell flyes in a wide arc to isolate the upper chest (pectorals) while one arm remains elevated as the other lowers. The movement emphasizes a controlled stretch and contraction of the upper chest with stabilization from the anterior delts and forearms.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Leg Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so your back is fully supported and your knees start around a 90° angle when feet are on the footplate; sit upright, feet about shoulder-width apart on the platform. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat so your back is fully supported and your knees start around a 90° angle when feet are on the footplate; sit upright, feet about shoulder-width apart on the platform.
Grip the side handles for stability and unlock the machine's safety latch if present so the sled can move freely.
Brace your core, press through your heels and mid-foot to extend your knees and hips, driving the platform away until legs are almost straight — do not lock the knees.

Pause briefly at the top under control, then inhale and slowly lower the platform by bending the knees until they return to about the starting 90° angle (or slightly deeper) while keeping the back flat against the pad.
Maintain even foot pressure (avoid lifting heels), keep controlled tempo, and breathe out on the pressing (concentric) phase and inhale on the lowering (eccentric) phase.
Rerack or re-engage the safety latch before releasing the handles at the end of the set; stop if you feel your hips rising off the pad or your knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: Seated leg press on a lever machine where you push a footplate away by extending the hips and knees; primarily develops knee extension strength in the quads while the glutes, hamstrings and calves assist. Performed seated with the back supported and feet placed on the angled platform.

exercises.name: Barbell Seated Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright near the edge of a flat bench with knees bent at about 90 degrees and feet flat on the floor (or on a small block for increased range of motion). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright near the edge of a flat bench with knees bent at about 90 degrees and feet flat on the floor (or on a small block for increased range of motion).
Place a barbell across your upper thighs just above the knees; use a pad if needed for comfort and grip the bar with both hands to steady it (seated position).
Position the balls of your feet on the floor (or a small raised platform) while keeping heels hanging or supported so your ankles can move through full range.
Keeping your torso upright and core engaged, exhale and press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, squeezing the calves at the top.

Pause briefly at the top with the calves fully contracted, then inhale and slowly lower the heels back down until you feel a stretch in the calves but maintain control (avoid letting the weight slam down).
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; maintain a steady tempo, avoid bouncing, and keep knees stationary throughout the set.
To stop, carefully roll the barbell forward off your thighs or have a spotter help lift it off before standing.
exercises.description: Seated on a bench with a loaded barbell resting across the thighs, raise the heels by plantarflexing the ankles to target the calf muscles. The movement is performed with knees bent, emphasizing the soleus portion of the calves through controlled heel raises and lowers.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.

Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Lever Deadlift (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Approach the machine and hinge at the hips while maintaining a neutral spine; bend knees until you can grasp the handles with a firm overhand or mixed grip. Stop 1 rep short of failure (RPE 9). Spinal safety takes priority.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width to slightly wider, toes pointing forward or slightly outward, with the lever bar/handles positioned over mid-foot.
Approach the machine and hinge at the hips while maintaining a neutral spine; bend knees until you can grasp the handles with a firm overhand or mixed grip.
Set your chest up, pull shoulder blades slightly back, keep gaze forward and core braced; shoulders should be roughly over or slightly in front of the handles.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips and knees simultaneously to lift the lever, focusing on squeezing the glutes at full standing position; keep the spine neutral throughout.
Pause briefly at the top with hips fully extended (no hyperextension of the lower back), then control the descent by pushing hips back and bending knees to lower the lever back to the start.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining steady breathing (exhale on the drive up, inhale on the lowering) and avoid rounding the lower back or jerking the weight.
Adjust foot position, grip width, and load as needed; ensure machine safety stops or latches are set before beginning heavier sets.
exercises.description: Plate-loaded lever deadlift performed from a standing hip-hinge position; focuses on driving the hips forward to extend the legs and lift the lever, targeting the glutes and posterior chain. The movement uses a fixed lever arm and handles rather than a free barbell.

exercises.name: Barbell Floor Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Load a barbell and position it across the upper back/trapezius, not the neck; grip the bar just outside shoulder width with a firm hold. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Load a barbell and position it across the upper back/trapezius, not the neck; grip the bar just outside shoulder width with a firm hold.
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and weight evenly distributed on the mid-foot; keep a slight bend in the knees (not locked).
Brace your core and maintain an upright torso; focus on keeping shoulders down and the bar stable on your traps/delts.
Slowly raise both heels by pushing through the balls of your feet until you reach full plantarflexion (stand on your toes); contract the calves at the top and hold for 1–2 seconds.
Lower your heels back to the floor in a controlled manner until the soles are flat, feeling a stretch in the calves.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the ascent and inhaling on the descent.
If balance is an issue, perform the movement near a wall or use a spotter; avoid bouncing or using excessive forward trunk lean to lift the weight.
exercises.description: Standing barbell calf raise performed with a barbell resting on the upper back to load the lower legs; raise the heels by plantarflexing the ankles, pause at the top, then lower with control. This exercise primarily isolates the gastrocnemius/soleus (calves) while the upper back and shoulders stabilize the load.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Lever Horizontal Leg Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so your knees start bent at about 90 degrees (or slightly less) without pinching the hip; grip the side handles for stability. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the machine with your back fully supported against the pad and feet hip-width on the footplate (mid-foot to whole foot contact).
Adjust the seat so your knees start bent at about 90 degrees (or slightly less) without pinching the hip; grip the side handles for stability.
Release any safety latch or lock that holds the lever in place before beginning the set.
Inhale, then press the footplate forward by extending your knees and hips until legs are nearly straight; avoid locking the knees at full extension.
Exhale as you drive the plate away, pushing through your heels to emphasize the quads and glutes.
Pause briefly at the top with tension maintained, then slowly bend the knees to return the plate to the start position under control.
Keep your lower back pressed into the pad throughout; do not let the hips lift off the seat or allow the knees to collapse inward.
After completing the set, secure the lever with the safety lock before exiting the machine.
exercises.description: Seated horizontal leg press performed on a lever plate to extend the knees against resistance, primarily targeting the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and calves. The back remains supported on an angled seat while the feet push the footplate forward.

exercises.name: Barbell Straight Leg Deadlift
exercises.notes: Start standing (upright) with the barbell on the floor or racked at mid-thigh; feet about hip-width apart and toes pointing forward or slightly out. Stop 1 rep short of failure (RPE 9). Spinal safety takes priority.
instructions: Start standing (upright) with the barbell on the floor or racked at mid-thigh; feet about hip-width apart and toes pointing forward or slightly out.
Grip the bar with a shoulder-width overhand (pronated) grip and stand tall so the bar hangs at arm's length in front of your thighs.
Set a slight bend in the knees (do not lock them) and brace your core so your spine is neutral; keep the chest up and shoulders back.
Begin the movement by hinging at the hips: push your hips straight back while keeping the bar close to your legs and maintaining the slight knee bend.
Lower the bar until you feel a strong but controlled stretch in the hamstrings (typically to mid-shin or just below the knees), keeping the back flat and head in a neutral line.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the hamstrings and core—do not let the lower back round.
To return, drive the hips forward forcefully, contract the glutes, and stand tall while keeping the bar close to the body; exhale as you rise.
At the top, avoid hyperextending the lower back—finish with a neutral spine and hips fully closed.
Perform controlled repetitions, focusing on hip hinge mechanics and grip; if grip fails before the hamstrings, use straps or lighter weight.
exercises.description: A hip-hinge barbell lift performed with minimal knee bend to emphasize the hamstrings and posterior chain; the movement lowers the bar by pushing the hips back and returns by driving the hips forward and squeezing the glutes.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Hip Extension
exercises.notes: Set an appropriate weight on the lever machine and position the ankle/calf cuff or pad behind the working lower leg (just above the heel/ankle). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an appropriate weight on the lever machine and position the ankle/calf cuff or pad behind the working lower leg (just above the heel/ankle).
Stand facing the machine support and lean forward, placing your forearms or chest against the pad/handles so your torso is stable and supported (standing position, body braced).
Place your non-working foot firmly on the floor for support and keep the support-side knee slightly bent to stabilize; the working knee can remain slightly bent as it moves.
Begin with the working thigh roughly in line with your torso (neutral hip). Maintain a neutral spine and avoid arching or rotating the pelvis throughout the movement.
Drive the working leg backward by extending the hip, squeezing the glutes at the top of the movement until the leg is behind the body (do not force hyperextension).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining pelvic stability, then slowly control the lever back to the start position until the hip returns to neutral.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then switch legs. Keep movement slow and controlled and do not use momentum from the torso or swinging of the leg.
exercises.description: A machine-based single-leg hip extension performed standing with the torso braced on a support; the working leg pushes a lever backward to extend the hip and drive the leg behind the body, emphasizing glute contraction.

exercises.name: Lever Sitting Toe Raise
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the footplate with your heels hanging off the edge and grasp the machine handles or sides of the bench for stability. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the machine bench with your knees bent at about 90 degrees and the padded lever resting across your lower thighs just above the knees.
Place the balls of your feet on the footplate with your heels hanging off the edge and grasp the machine handles or sides of the bench for stability.
Start with your heels lowered to feel a stretch in the calves (ankles dorsiflexed).
Exhale and press down through the balls of your feet, plantarflexing the ankles to raise your heels as high as possible while keeping the knees stationary.
Hold the top position for a brief pause, squeezing the calves, then slowly lower your heels back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping a controlled tempo and maintaining upright torso and stable knee position throughout.
exercises.description: A seated calf-raise performed on a lever-style machine that isolates the lower leg by plantarflexing the ankle against resistance while the knees are bent. It emphasizes the soleus and gastrocnemius to build calf strength and size.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.
