exercises.name: Barbell Clean grip Front Squat
exercises.notes: Set up the barbell in a front-rack position using a clean grip: hands just outside shoulders, fingers under the bar, elbows lifted so upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor; stand with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set up the barbell in a front-rack position using a clean grip: hands just outside shoulders, fingers under the bar, elbows lifted so upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor; stand with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out.
Brace your core and keep chest up and back straight (neutral spine); eyes forward and shoulders high to support the bar on the front deltoids/upper chest.
Inhale, initiate the movement by bending at the hips and knees simultaneously, pushing the knees outward to track over the toes and keeping the elbows up so the bar does not roll forward.
Descend under control until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or to a comfortable depth while maintaining an upright torso), keeping weight distributed through the midfoot and heels.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining core tension and a proud chest, then drive the feet into the ground (focus on heels) to extend the knees and hips and return to standing.
Exhale as you stand up, fully extending hips and knees while keeping elbows high and the bar stable on your shoulders; reset breathing and posture before the next rep.

Keep repetitions controlled and avoid letting the torso collapse forward, the elbows drop, or the knees cave inward; if wrist discomfort occurs with the clean grip, adjust hand position or use cross-arm/front-rack variation.
exercises.description: A front-loaded barbell squat performed with a clean grip (bar rests on the front shoulders, elbows high) that emphasizes knee extension to target the quadriceps while also engaging the posterior chain and core for stability. Maintain an upright torso and high elbows throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Lever Lying Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Set the lever machine so the pivot aligns with the knee joint and place the lower-leg pad just above the heels / over the Achilles area. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the lever machine so the pivot aligns with the knee joint and place the lower-leg pad just above the heels / over the Achilles area.
Lie prone (face down) on the bench with your hips flat on the pad, chest supported, and hands holding the front handles or bench for stability.
Position your legs fully extended with the pad resting on the back of your lower legs and feet relaxed; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Exhale and curl your lower legs by flexing the knees, bringing your heels toward your glutes in a controlled motion until you reach near-full knee flexion.
Pause and squeeze the hamstrings at the top for 1 second, keeping hips and torso pressed into the bench (avoid lifting the hips).
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the start (near full knee extension) under control without letting the weight slam or locking the knees.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining steady tempo and full range of motion; adjust load so you can control both concentric and eccentric phases.
Stop or reduce weight if you feel excessive hip rise, lower-back strain, or knee pain; keep movements smooth and joint-focused.
exercises.description: Prone (lying face down) leg curl performed on a lever machine that isolates the hamstrings by flexing the knee against a padded lever. The movement curls the lower leg toward the buttocks while the torso remains stabilized on the bench.

exercises.name: Barbell Deadlift
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and push them back while keeping a slight bend in the knees; reach down and grasp the bar with hands just outside the knees (mixed or double overhand grip). Stop 1 rep short of failure (RPE 9). Spinal safety takes priority.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart, toes pointing forward or slightly out, barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips and push them back while keeping a slight bend in the knees; reach down and grasp the bar with hands just outside the knees (mixed or double overhand grip).
Set a neutral spine (no rounding), pull shoulder blades down and back, lift the chest and brace the abs tightly.
Take a deep breath, drive through the heels and mid-foot, extend the hips and knees together to lift the bar, keeping the bar close to the shins and thighs.
Continue driving the hips forward until standing tall with hips fully extended and shoulders stacked over hips (lockout); do not hyperextend the lower back.
Pause briefly at the top with glutes engaged and core braced.
To lower, hinge at the hips first by pushing them back while maintaining a neutral spine, let the bar pass the knees, then bend the knees and place the bar back on the floor under control.
Reset breathing and core tension before each repetition and keep consistent form; avoid rounding the spine and jerking the bar.
exercises.description: A heavy compound hip-hinge lift that develops the glutes and the posterior chain by lifting a loaded barbell from the floor to a standing position. The movement also requires strong spinal bracing and upper-back stability while challenging grip strength.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Raise (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the seated calf raise machine so your knees are bent at about 90 degrees and your back is upright (seated position).
Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
Position the padded lever across your thighs just above the knees and load the desired plates on the lever (plate-loaded).
Grasp the handles or machine grips for stability and brace your core.
Begin with your heels lowered until you feel a stretch in the calves (ankle dorsiflexion).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible (ankle plantarflexion), lifting the lever and plates.
Pause and squeeze your calves at the top for 1–2 seconds while keeping knees bent and torso stable.
Lower your heels slowly and under control back to the starting stretched position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding bouncing or using hip/knee extension to assist the movement.
exercises.description: Seated, plate-loaded calf raise that isolates the calf musculature by plantarflexing the ankles while the knees are bent; the lever pad rests on the thighs and weight is applied via a plate-loaded lever. Performed from a seated position to emphasize the soleus and overall calf development.

exercises.name: Lever Sitting Toe Raise
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the footplate with your heels hanging off the edge and grasp the machine handles or sides of the bench for stability. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the machine bench with your knees bent at about 90 degrees and the padded lever resting across your lower thighs just above the knees.
Place the balls of your feet on the footplate with your heels hanging off the edge and grasp the machine handles or sides of the bench for stability.
Start with your heels lowered to feel a stretch in the calves (ankles dorsiflexed).
Exhale and press down through the balls of your feet, plantarflexing the ankles to raise your heels as high as possible while keeping the knees stationary.
Hold the top position for a brief pause, squeezing the calves, then slowly lower your heels back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping a controlled tempo and maintaining upright torso and stable knee position throughout.
exercises.description: A seated calf-raise performed on a lever-style machine that isolates the lower leg by plantarflexing the ankle against resistance while the knees are bent. It emphasizes the soleus and gastrocnemius to build calf strength and size.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Leg Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so your back is fully supported and your knees start around a 90° angle when feet are on the footplate; sit upright, feet about shoulder-width apart on the platform. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat so your back is fully supported and your knees start around a 90° angle when feet are on the footplate; sit upright, feet about shoulder-width apart on the platform.
Grip the side handles for stability and unlock the machine's safety latch if present so the sled can move freely.
Brace your core, press through your heels and mid-foot to extend your knees and hips, driving the platform away until legs are almost straight — do not lock the knees.

Pause briefly at the top under control, then inhale and slowly lower the platform by bending the knees until they return to about the starting 90° angle (or slightly deeper) while keeping the back flat against the pad.
Maintain even foot pressure (avoid lifting heels), keep controlled tempo, and breathe out on the pressing (concentric) phase and inhale on the lowering (eccentric) phase.
Rerack or re-engage the safety latch before releasing the handles at the end of the set; stop if you feel your hips rising off the pad or your knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: Seated leg press on a lever machine where you push a footplate away by extending the hips and knees; primarily develops knee extension strength in the quads while the glutes, hamstrings and calves assist. Performed seated with the back supported and feet placed on the angled platform.

exercises.name: Barbell Glute Bridge (hands on bar)
exercises.notes: Lie supine on the floor with knees bent and feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart; position the loaded barbell across your hips and grasp the bar with both hands on either side to stabilize it. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Lie supine on the floor with knees bent and feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart; position the loaded barbell across your hips and grasp the bar with both hands on either side to stabilize it.

Brace your core (engage abs) and press your heels into the floor so that your shins are roughly vertical or slightly angled; keep your chin neutral and your upper back and shoulders resting on the floor.

Drive through your heels and squeeze your glutes to lift your hips off the floor, extending the hips until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees (avoid hyperextending the lower back).
Hold the top position briefly and actively squeeze the glutes, keeping the bar stable with your hands and maintaining a neutral spine.
Slowly lower your hips back down under control until your glutes touch or are just above the floor, resetting your core tension as needed.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining even foot placement and steady breathing (exhale on the lift, inhale on the descent).
exercises.description: Supine hip thrust variation with a barbell placed across the hips; drive the hips upward by contracting the glutes until the body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees. Hands hold the bar at the hips to stabilize the load while the core and posterior chain control the movement.

exercises.name: Lever Leg Extension (Inward Toes)
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the leg extension machine with your back firmly against the back pad and adjust the seat so the machine's knee pivot lines up with your knee joint. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on the leg extension machine with your back firmly against the back pad and adjust the seat so the machine's knee pivot lines up with your knee joint.
Position the lower pad just above your ankles and place your feet on the footpad with your toes turned inward (feet slightly internally rotated). Grip the side handles or seat for stability.
Start with knees bent at about 90 degrees (or the machine's start position). Brace your core and keep your spine neutral; do not arch your lower back.
Exhale and extend your knees, pushing the pad upward until your legs are nearly straight but avoid locking the knees at full extension.
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the quads, then inhale and lower the weight slowly and with control back to the start position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining the inward toe position and steady tempo throughout. Stop if you feel sharp knee pain and ensure the movement is smooth and controlled.
exercises.description: Seated leg extension performed on a lever/machine with the toes turned inward; the movement isolates knee extension to target the quadriceps while the inward toe position slightly alters quad activation. The torso stays seated against the back pad and the lower shins press into the machine pad as you extend and lower the legs under control.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles.
Adjust the thigh/pelvic pad so it rests snugly on top of your thighs to prevent your hips from rising during the movement; grip the handles for stability.
Start with knees nearly extended but not fully locked, torso upright and core engaged to stabilize the spine.
Exhale and curl the lower leg by flexing the knees, driving the padded roller down and back toward your glutes in a controlled motion.
Pause and squeeze the hamstrings at the top of the movement without lifting your hips off the seat.
Inhale and slowly return to the start position, fully extending the knees under control without letting the weight slam or locking the knees.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping the torso still and avoiding swinging or using momentum.
exercises.description: Seated leg curl on a lever machine isolates knee flexion to train the hamstrings, with assistance from the glutes and calves. Performed seated with a padded lever that is pulled by bending the knees and bringing the heels toward the buttocks.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Set the desired weight on the lever standing calf raise machine and step onto the foot platform so the balls of your feet are on the edge and your heels can hang freely. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the desired weight on the lever standing calf raise machine and step onto the foot platform so the balls of your feet are on the edge and your heels can hang freely.
Position your shoulders under the padded lever, stand tall and grip the machine handles for stability; feet should be hip-width and toes pointing forward (or slightly turned in/out to vary emphasis).
Begin with a full stretch: lower your heels down until you feel a controlled stretch in the calves (keep torso upright and spine neutral).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, exhaling and keeping knees straight (a slight knee bend shifts emphasis to the soleus if desired).
Pause 1–2 seconds at the top and squeeze the calves, then slowly lower your heels back to the stretched starting position with control—avoid bouncing.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and proper alignment throughout.
exercises.description: A machine-based standing calf raise where the shoulders are braced under a padded lever and the heels drop and rise from a platform to load the calf muscles through a full range of motion. Emphasizes plantarflexion to strengthen and develop the gastrocnemius/soleus complex.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Pause Front Squat
exercises.notes: Brace your core and keep a tall, neutral spine and chest up; pull your shoulder blades together to engage the upper back and traps. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out, barbell resting on the front of your shoulders in a secure front rack (clean grip or cross-arm) with elbows high.
Brace your core and keep a tall, neutral spine and chest up; pull your shoulder blades together to engage the upper back and traps.
Inhale and descend by bending at the hips and knees, sending knees out to track over your toes and keeping weight distributed through the midfoot and heels.
Lower until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or slightly below) while maintaining upright torso and high elbows.
Hold a controlled pause at the bottom for 1–3 seconds without relaxing your core or letting the knees collapse inward.
Drive forcefully through the heels and midfoot, extend the hips and knees to stand back up while exhaling, keeping elbows high and chest lifted.
Reset your breath and positioning at the top before beginning the next repetition; choose a pause duration and weight that allow strict form throughout.
exercises.description: A front-loaded barbell squat performed with a controlled pause at the bottom to remove momentum and increase time under tension; emphasizes upright torso and knee extension to target the quadriceps. Pause at the bottom for 1–3 seconds before driving up through the heels to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Sumo Deadlift
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower into a squat-like setup (upright torso, hips lower than a conventional deadlift) and grip the bar with hands inside your knees using a double overhand or mixed grip. Stop 1 rep short of failure (RPE 9). Spinal safety takes priority.
instructions: Stand with feet well outside shoulder width (sumo stance), toes pointed slightly outward; position the bar over the mid-foot while standing upright.

Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower into a squat-like setup (upright torso, hips lower than a conventional deadlift) and grip the bar with hands inside your knees using a double overhand or mixed grip.
Brace your core and keep a neutral spine (no rounded back); pull your shoulder blades down and back so the lats are engaged and chest is up.
Drive your knees out so they track over your toes (toes and knees aligned) to engage the adductors and maintain the wide stance.
Initiate the lift by pushing through the heels and mid-foot, driving the hips forward and straightening the legs simultaneously—keep the bar close to the shins.
Stand tall at lockout by fully extending the hips and knees, squeezing the glutes and keeping the shoulders back (avoid hyperextending the lower back).
Lower the bar under control by hinging at the hips first, then bending the knees to return the bar to the floor while maintaining a neutral spine and tight core.
Reset your position between reps as needed (re-brace core, realign knees over toes, and ensure bar remains over mid-foot) before initiating the next repetition.
exercises.description: The barbell sumo deadlift is a wide-stance deadlift variation that emphasizes hip extension and inner-thigh drive to lift the bar from the floor. It uses a more upright torso and wider foot placement to shift load onto the glutes and adductors while still recruiting the posterior chain and legs.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles.
Adjust the thigh/pelvic pad so it rests snugly on top of your thighs to prevent your hips from rising during the movement; grip the handles for stability.
Start with knees nearly extended but not fully locked, torso upright and core engaged to stabilize the spine.
Exhale and curl the lower leg by flexing the knees, driving the padded roller down and back toward your glutes in a controlled motion.
Pause and squeeze the hamstrings at the top of the movement without lifting your hips off the seat.
Inhale and slowly return to the start position, fully extending the knees under control without letting the weight slam or locking the knees.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping the torso still and avoiding swinging or using momentum.
exercises.description: Seated leg curl on a lever machine isolates knee flexion to train the hamstrings, with assistance from the glutes and calves. Performed seated with a padded lever that is pulled by bending the knees and bringing the heels toward the buttocks.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Raise (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the seated calf raise machine so your knees are bent at about 90 degrees and your back is upright (seated position).
Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
Position the padded lever across your thighs just above the knees and load the desired plates on the lever (plate-loaded).
Grasp the handles or machine grips for stability and brace your core.
Begin with your heels lowered until you feel a stretch in the calves (ankle dorsiflexion).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible (ankle plantarflexion), lifting the lever and plates.
Pause and squeeze your calves at the top for 1–2 seconds while keeping knees bent and torso stable.
Lower your heels slowly and under control back to the starting stretched position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding bouncing or using hip/knee extension to assist the movement.
exercises.description: Seated, plate-loaded calf raise that isolates the calf musculature by plantarflexing the ankles while the knees are bent; the lever pad rests on the thighs and weight is applied via a plate-loaded lever. Performed from a seated position to emphasize the soleus and overall calf development.

exercises.name: Lever Banded Abdominal Leg Raise
exercises.notes: Set up on a vertical knee-raise / captain's-chair station: place your forearms on the pad, grip the handles, press your lower back against the back pad and let your legs hang straight down. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set up on a vertical knee-raise / captain's-chair station: place your forearms on the pad, grip the handles, press your lower back against the back pad and let your legs hang straight down.
If using a band or lever attachment, anchor the band to the foot/lever according to the machine's setup so resistance is applied to the legs during the raise.
Brace your core by drawing the belly button toward the spine and maintain a neutral head and neck position throughout.
Exhale and slowly raise your knees (or straight legs for a harder variation) upward toward your chest by contracting the abs, keeping the movement controlled and avoiding momentum.
Pause for 1–2 seconds at the top with hips slightly flexed and ribs down to emphasize the abdominal contraction.
Inhale and slowly lower the legs back to the starting hanging position with control, resisting the band/lever on the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping the lower back pressed to the pad and minimizing swinging; adjust band tension or lever setting to increase or decrease resistance.
exercises.description: A vertical leg-raise performed on a captain's-chair / lever station with banded resistance that primarily trains the abdominal muscles by lifting the legs toward the torso while the back and forearms are supported. The band/lever provides added resistance or assistance through the movement.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.

exercises.name: Lever Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine so the back pad is set to an incline (approximately 30–45°) and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the machine so the back pad is set to an incline (approximately 30–45°) and select an appropriate weight.
Sit with your back pressed firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor, chest lifted and shoulders down; grasp the handles with a neutral grip and arms out to the sides at roughly chest height.
Slightly bend your elbows and retract/depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulders before starting the movement.
Exhale and drive the handles upward and inward in a controlled, wide arc, leading with the elbows while maintaining the slight elbow bend.
Bring the handles together above the upper chest and hold a brief squeeze (about 1 second) focusing on contracting the pectorals.

Inhale and slowly reverse the motion, resisting the weight as you lower the handles back to the starting position until you feel a controlled stretch in the chest—do not let the shoulders shrug or the elbows fully lock.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding momentum or excessive range that causes shoulder discomfort.
exercises.description: Seated incline lever fly performed on an incline lever machine to isolate the upper portion of the pectorals by moving the arms in a wide arc while the torso is supported on an incline pad.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.

Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise
exercises.notes: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
instructions: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
Attach wrist straps to low pulley handles (both sides) and set a light–moderate weight so you can control the entire range.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the bench, and grasp the wrist straps so your arms hang down in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows; palms should face each other.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

instructions: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees slightly soft (not locked).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or slightly neutral and elbows roughly under the wrists.
Brace your core and squeeze your glutes to maintain a neutral spine and prevent lower-back arching.
Exhale and press both dumbbells straight overhead in a controlled path until your arms are nearly fully extended (avoid jamming the elbows).
Keep the wrists stacked over the elbows and the chin tucked slightly; do not overextend the neck or lower back at the top.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and scapular stabilizers.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting shoulder-height position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining core tension and full control on both ascent and descent.

exercises.name: Dumbbell 2 Point Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back and shoulders supported and one foot planted firmly on the floor near the bench.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back and shoulders supported and one foot planted firmly on the floor near the bench.

Bend the opposite knee and lift that foot off the floor so only one foot is providing support (single‑leg position); press your hips up into a bridge so your torso forms a straight line from shoulder to knee and brace your abs.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand (or a single dumbbell if using one) at chest level with a neutral grip or palms facing forward, elbows bent and tucked at about a 45° angle to your torso.
Inhale, then press the weight straight up by extending your arms until elbows are nearly locked and the dumbbells are over your chest while keeping hips elevated and core tight.
Pause briefly at the top, keeping shoulders stable on the bench and hips high, then slowly lower the dumbbells back to your chest under control while maintaining the bridge position.
Complete the prescribed reps on one leg, then carefully lower your hips, switch legs, and repeat on the other side. Keep movement controlled and avoid hyperextending the lower back.
exercises.description: A bench press performed while holding a hip bridge (single-leg position) to add core and glute stability demand; you press the dumbbells from chest to full arm extension while maintaining an elevated hip. This combines chest pressing with unilateral lower‑body stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise

Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press (parallel grip)
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back fully supported, feet flat on the floor and core braced (seated, back-supported).
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back fully supported, feet flat on the floor and core braced (seated, back-supported).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with a parallel grip (palms facing each other), elbows roughly under wrists and pointing slightly forward.
Keep the chest up and shoulder blades stable; exhale and press the dumbbells straight up until the arms are nearly extended but not locked.
Pause briefly at the top with wrists neutral and head in a neutral position, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive lower back arching or flaring the elbows too wide.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press with a parallel (palms-facing) grip; presses dumbbells from shoulder height overhead to work the delts while the triceps and upper back stabilize and assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Face Pull (with rope)
exercises.notes: Set a rope handle on a high pulley (about head/eye level) and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a rope handle on a high pulley (about head/eye level) and select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright facing the machine with feet flat on the floor and knees bent (or seated on a bench); keep a tall spine and neutral head position.
Grasp the rope with a neutral grip (thumbs pointing toward you) and extend your arms so there is tension on the cable.
Initiate the movement by pulling the rope toward your face, leading with your elbows and keeping them at about shoulder height.
As you pull, externally rotate your shoulders so the ends of the rope separate and your hands move either side of your face; squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end range.
Pause briefly at peak contraction, keeping the torso stable and avoiding excessive backward lean or shrugging.
Slowly return the rope to the start position with control, maintaining tension in the cable and scapular control throughout.
Repeat for the desired number of reps and maintain controlled tempo to protect the shoulder joint.
exercises.description: A seated cable face pull using a rope attachment that emphasizes the rear delts and upper posterior shoulder musculature by pulling the rope toward the face while externally rotating the hands and squeezing the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Rear Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so you can lie prone (face down) with your chest supported and feet on the floor or toes touching for stability.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so you can lie prone (face down) with your chest supported and feet on the floor or toes touching for stability.
Pick a pair of light-to-moderate dumbbells and hold one in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other). Let your arms hang straight down toward the floor.
Bend your knees slightly if needed and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down) so your spine stays aligned with the bench.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together (scapular retraction) and raising your arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows, leading with the elbows, not the hands.
Lift until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor or in line with your shoulders, feeling the emphasis on the rear delts and upper back; avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping tension on the muscles, then slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start under control without letting the torso rotate or the weights drop.
Repeat for the desired reps, focusing on controlled movement and scapular control rather than momentum; reduce weight if you must swing or arch the lower back.
exercises.description: Prone on an incline bench, this rear fly variation isolates the posterior delts by raising the arms out to the sides while keeping the torso supported, emphasizing scapular retraction and upper‑back activation.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Pec Fly
exercises.notes: Set the lever machine handles so they are roughly at mid-chest/shoulder height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set the lever machine handles so they are roughly at mid-chest/shoulder height and select an appropriate weight.
Stand centered between the lever arms with feet about shoulder-width apart and a soft bend in the knees; maintain a neutral spine and a slight forward lean from the hips.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-in grip and raise your arms out to the sides so they are roughly parallel to the floor; maintain a slight bend in the elbows throughout (start position).
Exhale and bring the handles together in front of your chest by squeezing the pectorals, keeping the elbow angle constant and avoiding shoulder shrugging or excessive forward elbow travel.
Pause briefly at peak contraction with hands meeting or close in front of the chest, focusing on contracting the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly control the levers back to the start position until you feel a stretch across the chest; do not let the weights slam back.
Repeat for the prescribed reps. Keep shoulders down and back, core braced, and avoid rotating the torso or using momentum.
exercises.description: A standing machine lever pec fly that isolates the chest by bringing two arms together in front of the body from a wide, horizontal start. Performed standing with a slight forward lean to maximize pectoral contraction while the delts and serratus anterior assist and stabilize.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Face Pull
exercises.notes: Set a cable pulley to about eye- to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
instructions: Set a cable pulley to about eye- to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
Stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart or in a slight staggered stance, knees soft and spine neutral.
Grasp the rope with both hands using a neutral/overhand grip (palms facing inward) and take a step back so there is tension on the cable.
Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the rope toward your face, driving the elbows out to the sides so your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
As you reach the end range, externally rotate the shoulders so the hands split apart and the rope ends are near your temples or ears; pause and squeeze the rear delts and upper back.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, extending the arms forward while maintaining scapular control until the hands return to the start position.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping torso stable (no excessive leaning or momentum) and focusing on quality of movement and external rotation.
exercises.description: A standing cable face pull with a rope attachment that emphasizes the posterior delts and upper back by pulling the rope toward the face while externally rotating the shoulders. It develops rear shoulder strength, scapular retraction, and shoulder external rotators.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Goblet Squat
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out, holding a single dumbbell vertically at chest level (both hands cupping one end) — this is the goblet position.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out, holding a single dumbbell vertically at chest level (both hands cupping one end) — this is the goblet position.
Brace your core and keep a neutral spine and chest lifted; elbows should point down between your knees as you move.
Initiate the movement by sending hips back and bending the knees, keeping weight distributed through the heels and mid-foot; push knees out in line with toes to avoid collapse.
Descend until your thighs are approximately parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while maintaining an upright torso and the dumbbell close to your chest.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then push through the heels to extend the hips and knees and return to standing, keeping the core engaged and the dumbbell steady.
Repeat for the desired reps; inhale on the way down and exhale as you drive up. Avoid letting the heels rise or knees cave inward.
exercises.description: A goblet squat performed standing while holding a dumbbell at the chest; it emphasizes knee-driven squat movement to load the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Single Leg Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Start standing upright (single-leg stance) on the working foot with toes facing forward; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (or one dumbbell in each hand if preferred) and let the non-working leg hang or be slightly bent behind you -- torso neutral and shoulders relaxed.
instructions: Start standing upright (single-leg stance) on the working foot with toes facing forward; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (or one dumbbell in each hand if preferred) and let the non-working leg hang or be slightly bent behind you -- torso neutral and shoulders relaxed.
Find balance and keep the knee of the working leg straight or with a slight bend (do not lock); engage your abs to stabilize the torso and keep weight centered over the midfoot.
Slowly raise your heel by plantarflexing the ankle (push through the ball of the foot) until you reach maximum height on the toes; avoid leaning forward or using momentum.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the calf, maintaining an upright posture and stable core.
Lower the heel slowly and with control until the sole is flat on the floor (or slightly below the step if performed on an elevated surface) to complete one repetition.
Perform the desired number of reps, then switch legs; use a nearby wall or rail for light support if balance is a limiting factor but avoid pushing off with the support.
exercises.description: A single-leg calf raise performed standing while holding dumbbell(s) to add resistance; it isolates the calf (gastrocnemius/soleus) on the working leg while the opposite leg is off the ground. Balance and core stability are required to perform controlled raises and lowers.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Squat
exercises.notes: Step 1: Set a flat bench behind you and load the barbell in a squat rack at about mid-chest height; step under the bar and position it across your upper back/traps (not on the neck).
instructions: Step 1: Set a flat bench behind you and load the barbell in a squat rack at about mid-chest height; step under the bar and position it across your upper back/traps (not on the neck).
Step 2: Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder width, lift the chest, brace your abs and unrack the bar by standing up, then step back 1–2 foot lengths until the bench is directly behind you.
Step 3: Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core throughout.
Step 4: Begin the descent by pushing your hips back slightly and bending the knees, keeping weight through the midfoot/heels and knees tracking over your toes.
Step 5: Lower under control until your glutes lightly touch the bench (brief contact only) — do not fully sit or relax on the bench.
Step 6: Drive through the heels and extend the hips and knees to return to standing, exhaling as you rise and keeping the chest up.
Step 7: Step forward to re-rack the bar safely when finished; use a spotter for heavy loads and avoid bouncing off the bench or rounding the lower back.
exercises.description: A bench (box) squat variation performed with a barbell across the upper back where you sit back to a bench to control depth, emphasizing the quadriceps while also engaging the posterior chain and core for stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, feet about hip‑width apart and toes pointing forward.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, feet about hip‑width apart and toes pointing forward.
Set a slight bend in the knees (soft knees) and brace your core to stabilize the spine before initiating the movement.

Hinge at the hips by pushing your hips straight back while keeping the spine neutral and chest slightly lifted; allow the dumbbells to slide down the front of your thighs and shins, staying close to the body.
Lower until you feel a strong stretch in the hamstrings or the dumbbells reach mid‑shin (maintain neutral cervical alignment, do not round the lower back).
Reverse the motion by driving the hips forward, squeezing the glutes and hamstrings to return to standing while keeping the knees softly bent and the weights close to the legs.
Maintain controlled tempo (slow on the descent, controlled drive on the ascent), keep the shoulders down away from the ears, and avoid using the lower back to lift.
exercises.description: A standing hip‑hinge exercise performed with dumbbells that emphasizes eccentric loading of the hamstrings while the hips move back and forth; the movement also engages the glutes and core to maintain a neutral spine. Keep a slight knee bend and the weights close to the legs throughout.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Single Leg Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (or one dumbbell in each hand if preferred), feet hip-width apart.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (or one dumbbell in each hand if preferred), feet hip-width apart.

Shift your weight onto one foot and lift the opposite foot slightly off the floor so you are balancing on a single leg (standing position, single-leg). Keep the supporting knee straight but not locked and torso tall.
Brace your core and maintain a neutral spine; if needed lightly touch a wall or hold a sturdy object for balance (wall-assisted) without leaning.

Slowly raise your heel by plantarflexing the ankle of the supporting leg until you are on the ball of your foot/toes, squeezing the calf at the top. Keep shoulders relaxed and dumbbells at your sides.
Pause 1–2 seconds at the top while maintaining balance and control.
Lower your heel slowly back to the starting position until the sole is flat on the floor (or slightly below the step edge if using a raised surface for increased range of motion).
Complete the desired number of repetitions on that leg, then switch to the other leg and repeat.
exercises.description: A single-leg standing calf raise with dumbbells isolates the calf (plantarflexors) on one side while challenging balance and stability. It builds strength and size in the calves while the core, grip, and leg stabilizers assist.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Lever Belt Sumo Squat
exercises.notes: Stand on the platform facing the machine with the lever belt secured low on your hips; grab the front handles for light support if available.
instructions: Stand on the platform facing the machine with the lever belt secured low on your hips; grab the front handles for light support if available.
Position your feet much wider than shoulder-width with toes pointed out at ~30–45 degrees (sumo stance).
Brace your core, keep chest tall and a neutral spine, and allow a slight forward torso lean while maintaining an upright upper body.

Begin the movement by pushing your hips back and bending at the knees, lowering yourself into a squat until your thighs are roughly parallel to the platform or a comfortable depth where knees track over toes.
Keep your knees tracking in line with your toes and distribute weight through your heels and mid-foot; avoid collapsing the knees inward.
Drive forcefully through the heels and hips to return to standing, fully extending the hips at the top while resisting the lever’s load throughout the motion.
Control the descent on each rep, breathe in on the way down and exhale as you drive up, and perform repetitions with a steady tempo.
exercises.description: A wide-stance (sumo) squat performed with a lever belt/harness attached to a machine; emphasizes hip drive and inner-thigh engagement while reducing load on the spine. Use a wide, turned-out foot position and sit back between the heels, then drive up through the hips.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.
