exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Zercher Back Extension
exercises.notes: Set up on a hyperextension / Roman chair: anchor your feet, position the hip pad under the front of your hips/upper thighs so your torso can hinge freely, and face down (prone).
instructions: Set up on a hyperextension / Roman chair: anchor your feet, position the hip pad under the front of your hips/upper thighs so your torso can hinge freely, and face down (prone).
Cradle a barbell in the crook of your elbows (Zercher hold) and keep the bar close to your body; cross your forearms or grip the bar to secure it and keep elbows tucked.
Start with your torso lowered toward the floor until you feel a stretch in the hamstrings and glutes or your body is roughly perpendicular to the pad (hip flexion). Keep a neutral neck and spine.

Drive your hips forward and contract your glutes and hamstrings to raise your torso until your body forms a straight line from head to heels or you reach a neutral spine — do not hyperextend the lower back.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tight core and glute contraction, then slowly lower back to the start position under control.
Breathe out as you extend up, inhale as you lower. Maintain tension in the hips and avoid bending at the knees or using momentum.
exercises.description: A hip-extension back extension performed on a Roman chair while holding a barbell in the Zercher (cradle of the elbows) position; it emphasizes hip drive to raise the torso and targets the glutes and hamstrings while the erector spinae stabilizes the spine.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

exercises.name: Hammer Grip Pull-up on Dip Cage
exercises.notes: Stand under the dip cage and reach up to grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), thumbs wrapped around the handles.
instructions: Stand under the dip cage and reach up to grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), thumbs wrapped around the handles.
If needed use the foot platform to step up, then remove foot support so your body hangs freely; legs can be slightly bent or crossed at the ankles to avoid touching the floor.
Start from a full dead hang with arms fully extended, shoulders down and back, chest slightly lifted and core braced to prevent swinging.
Exhale and pull your elbows down and back, driving the elbows toward your ribcage and squeezing the lats until your chin clears the bar or the handles.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the shoulders engaged and scapulae slightly retracted (pulled together).
Inhale and lower yourself slowly and with control back to a full hang, allowing the shoulders to extend but avoid shrugging excessively.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a steady tempo, minimal body momentum, and proper scapular control throughout.
Progression/modification: if a full bodyweight pull-up is too difficult, keep one foot on the platform for partial assistance or perform slow negatives from the top position.
exercises.description: Neutral (hammer) grip pull-up performed on the dip-cage handles that primarily targets the lats while recruiting the biceps, forearms and upper back. Hang from parallel handles and pull the torso upward until the chin clears the bar, then lower under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.notes: Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor while keeping a slight bend in both knees; maintain a neutral spine and long neck throughout.

exercises.name: Dumbbell One Arm Row (rack support)
exercises.notes: Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor while keeping a slight bend in both knees; maintain a neutral spine and long neck throughout.
instructions: Stand with a staggered stance (one foot forward, one foot back) beside a rack; place the hand nearest the rack on the rail at about hip height for support.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor while keeping a slight bend in both knees; maintain a neutral spine and long neck throughout.
Let the working arm hang straight down holding a dumbbell with a neutral or overhand grip, shoulder relaxed.
Brace your core and pull the dumbbell upward in a vertical path, leading with the elbow and driving it toward your ribcage/hip; keep the elbow close to your body.
Squeeze the shoulder blade toward the spine at the top of the movement and pause briefly to maximize lat/upper-back contraction.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position under control until the arm is fully extended, maintaining torso stability and avoiding rotation.
Complete the desired reps on one side, then switch sides so the opposite hand supports on the rack and repeat. Keep breathing steadily (exhale on the pull, inhale on the return).
exercises.description: A single-arm bent-over row performed with one hand bracing on a rack for support; the exercise emphasizes a unilateral pull to develop the lats while also engaging the upper back and arm muscles. The support hand and staggered stance increase stability and allow focus on a full, controlled contraction of the pulling side.

exercises.name: Reverse grip Pull-up
exercises.notes: Set up: grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower.
instructions: Set up: grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower.
Start position: hang fully extended with feet off the ground (legs can be crossed at the ankles or slightly bent) and scapula slightly depressed and retracted to remove slack.
Initiate the pull by driving the elbows down and back, engaging the lats and upper back rather than just curling with the arms.
Continue pulling until your chin passes the bar or your upper chest reaches the bar, keeping the torso as upright and controlled as possible.
Pause briefly at the top while squeezing the lats and upper back, avoiding excessive neck or shoulder shrugging.
Lower slowly and under control back to a full dead hang (arms fully extended) while maintaining scapular control.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Breathe out on the pull and inhale as you lower. Avoid kipping or excessive swinging to keep tension on the target muscles.
exercises.description: A supinated (underhand) grip pull-up that emphasizes the latissimus and recruits the biceps more than a pronated pull-up. From a dead hang you pull your torso up until your chin or chest reaches the bar, then lower with control.

exercises.name: Chest Dip
exercises.notes: Start standing between parallel bars or on the dip station and grasp the handles with a neutral grip; press down to lift your body so your arms are straight and feet are off the ground (legs can be bent or crossed behind you).
instructions: Start standing between parallel bars or on the dip station and grasp the handles with a neutral grip; press down to lift your body so your arms are straight and feet are off the ground (legs can be bent or crossed behind you).
Lean your torso forward about 20–30 degrees from vertical to shift emphasis onto the pectorals while keeping the core braced and scapulae slightly depressed.
Begin the descent by inhaling and bending the elbows, allowing them to track slightly outward (not flared excessively) as you lower your chest toward the level of the bars.
Lower until your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or your chest is just below the bar level, maintaining control and a forward lean throughout.
Exhale and press through your palms, extending the elbows to return to the start position with straight arms; avoid locking the elbows at the top.
Perform repetitions with a slow, controlled tempo; use an assistance machine or band, or reduce range of motion if you cannot maintain proper form.
exercises.description: A bodyweight dip performed on parallel bars with a forward lean to emphasize the chest (pectorals). The movement lowers the torso by bending the elbows and then presses the body back up to the start position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Cable seated row
exercises.notes: Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent (seated, upright position).
Grab the handle (V-bar or close grip) with a neutral or overhand grip and extend your arms so the weight stack is lifted and shoulders are protracted; maintain a neutral spine and chest slightly up.
Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
Keep the torso stable—avoid excessive leaning or using momentum; maintain a slight forward trunk angle only if required to reach the handle, then return to a tall, braced posture as you pull.

Squeeze the shoulder blades and lats at the end range for a brief 1–2 second pause, then slowly extend the arms forward under control to the start position while allowing the scapulae to move forward.
Breathe out while pulling and inhale while returning to the start; choose a weight that allows controlled form and full elbow extension between reps.
exercises.description: Seated cable row is a horizontal pulling exercise performed sitting with feet braced that primarily targets the lats while working the middle/upper back and arm pulling muscles. It emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive to pull the handle to the torso.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise
exercises.notes: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
instructions: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
Attach wrist straps to low pulley handles (both sides) and set a light–moderate weight so you can control the entire range.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the bench, and grasp the wrist straps so your arms hang down in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows; palms should face each other.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees slightly soft (not locked).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees slightly soft (not locked).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or slightly neutral and elbows roughly under the wrists.
Brace your core and squeeze your glutes to maintain a neutral spine and prevent lower-back arching.
Exhale and press both dumbbells straight overhead in a controlled path until your arms are nearly fully extended (avoid jamming the elbows).
Keep the wrists stacked over the elbows and the chin tucked slightly; do not overextend the neck or lower back at the top.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and scapular stabilizers.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting shoulder-height position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining core tension and full control on both ascent and descent.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press that primarily works the delts by pressing weights from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, while the triceps and upper-back muscles stabilize and assist.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Row
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulley to about chest height and attach a close-grip or double-handle.
instructions: Set the cable pulley to about chest height and attach a close-grip or double-handle.
Stand facing the machine with feet hip-width apart (or a slight staggered stance for balance) and a soft bend in the knees; grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip.
Step back until the cable is taut and hinge slightly at the hips so your torso is upright but tilted forward a few degrees; brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Begin the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together (scapular retraction), then drive your elbows straight back toward your sides, keeping them close to the body.
Pull until your hands reach your lower chest/upper abdomen and you feel a full contraction in the lats and upper back; avoid excessive torso lean or momentum.
Pause 0–1 seconds at the top while squeezing the shoulder blades, then slowly extend your arms back to the start under control, maintaining core tension.
Repeat for the desired reps and adjust weight so you can maintain strict form for the full set.
exercises.description: A standing cable rowing movement that targets the lats and mid/upper back by pulling a cable handle toward the torso while maintaining a braced, upright stance. Emphasizes scapular retraction and controlled concentric and eccentric phases.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Alternate Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and choose a pair of dumbbells you can control for the desired reps.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and choose a pair of dumbbells you can control for the desired reps.
Sit on the bench and plant your feet flat on the floor; lie back so your head, upper back and hips are supported (supine on an incline).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder level with elbows bent (~90°), wrists stacked over the elbows and palms facing forward or slightly neutral.
Brace your core and maintain a slight natural arch in the lower back; keep shoulder blades drawn lightly together and down.
Press one dumbbell upward and slightly toward the midline until the arm is nearly extended, exhaling on the press; avoid locking the elbow.
Lower that dumbbell slowly back to the start position while the opposite arm remains paused in the lowered/shoulder-height position.
Alternate and press the other dumbbell upward in the same controlled manner, inhaling as you lower each rep.
Continue alternating reps for the set, keeping movement controlled, wrists aligned, and avoiding excessive flaring of the elbows or letting the weights drop behind the head.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this exercise presses one dumbbell at a time upward in an alternating pattern to emphasize the upper portion of the chest while improving unilateral strength and stability.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable Bar Lateral Pulldown
exercises.notes: Setup: Select an appropriate weight, sit on the pulldown bench and secure your thighs under the knee pads so your legs are stable; feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Setup: Select an appropriate weight, sit on the pulldown bench and secure your thighs under the knee pads so your legs are stable; feet flat on the floor.
Grip: Reach up and take a wide, overhand grip on the lat pulldown bar (palms facing away) slightly wider than shoulder width.
Start position: Sit tall with a slight natural arch in the lower back, chest lifted, shoulders down and back (scapulae slightly retracted), and arms fully extended overhead holding the bar.
Initiate the pull: Exhale and pull your elbows down and back, driving the bar in a smooth arc toward the upper chest while keeping your torso relatively upright with only a slight backward lean.
Peak contraction: Pause briefly with the bar at or just below the collarbone/upper chest while squeezing the lats and retracting the shoulder blades.
Return: Inhale and slowly allow the bar to rise under control until your arms are fully extended and you feel a stretch in the lats; do not let the weight stack slam.

Form cues & safety: Avoid excessive torso swinging or using momentum, do not pull the bar behind the neck, keep wrists neutral and shoulders from shrugging, and maintain core bracing throughout the set.
Repetitions: Repeat for the desired number of reps with controlled tempo (e.g., 2 seconds down, 1–2 second pause, 2–3 seconds up).
exercises.description: Seated cable bar lateral pulldown that uses a wide overhand grip to pull a bar down toward the upper chest, emphasizing vertical adduction of the humerus to work the latissimus dorsi. The movement also recruits the biceps and upper back muscles to assist the pull.

exercises.name: Cable Rope Crossover Seated Row
exercises.notes: Set the low pulley and load an appropriate weight; sit on the bench or seat facing the machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent.
instructions: Set the low pulley and load an appropriate weight; sit on the bench or seat facing the machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Reach forward and grasp the rope with both hands using a neutral grip (palms facing each other) while keeping the spine neutral and chest open; start with arms fully extended.
Lean the torso slightly forward from the hips (not rounding the lower back) to establish a stable starting position.

Exhale and pull the rope toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and down, leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together at the end of the concentric phase.
At the peak contraction, optionally separate the rope ends slightly to increase rear delt and upper-back engagement, hold for 1–2 seconds while maintaining a braced core.
Inhale and slowly extend the arms back to the start position under control, maintaining scapular control and a neutral spine throughout.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions and avoid jerking the torso or using momentum; reduce weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: A seated cable row performed with a rope attachment that emphasizes scapular retraction and the upper back while also engaging the lats and posterior delts. The lifter sits facing the low pulley and pulls the rope toward the torso, squeezing the shoulder blades together.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Wide Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Step 1: Sit on the machine seat and adjust the thigh pads so your legs are secured and you cannot be lifted by the bar.
instructions: Step 1: Sit on the machine seat and adjust the thigh pads so your legs are secured and you cannot be lifted by the bar.
Step 2: Select an appropriate weight and reach up with a wide, pronated (palms facing forward) grip on the lever bar outside shoulder width.
Step 3: Sit upright with a slight natural arch in the lower back, chest lifted and a very slight lean back from the hips; keep feet flat on the floor.
Step 4: Brace your core, exhale, and initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and driving your elbows down and back to pull the bar toward the upper chest.
Step 5: Pause briefly at the bottom and squeeze the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Step 6: Inhale and slowly return the bar to the start position with controlled motion until the arms are nearly fully extended while maintaining tension in the lats.
Step 7: Repeat for the desired reps; avoid using momentum or excessive torso lean — keep movement controlled and driven by the back and elbow action.
exercises.description: A seated machine pulldown using a wide grip to emphasize the latissimus dorsi and upper back musculature; performed from a seated position with thigh pads to stabilize the torso. The movement pulls the bar down toward the upper chest while driving the elbows down and back.

exercises.name: Pull-up (neutral grip)
exercises.notes: Stand under the pull-up handles and reach up to grasp the parallel (neutral) grips with palms facing each other, hands about shoulder-width apart.
instructions: Stand under the pull-up handles and reach up to grasp the parallel (neutral) grips with palms facing each other, hands about shoulder-width apart.
Hang fully with arms extended, shoulders relaxed but slightly depressed and scapulae engaged (avoid hanging completely passive); cross or bend your legs to reduce swinging if needed.
Brace your core and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades down and together, then drive your elbows down toward your ribs while pulling your chest up toward the handles.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the level of your hands or your chest reaches the handles, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive kipping.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged, then lower yourself slowly and under control back to the full-hang position with arms extended.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining smooth controlled reps and avoiding momentum or excessive body swing.
exercises.description: A neutral-grip pull-up performed hanging from parallel handles with palms facing each other; you pull the torso upward until the chin clears the hands, emphasizing vertical pulling with strong lat engagement. This variation increases biceps involvement and is slightly easier on the shoulders than a wide pronated grip.

exercises.name: Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
instructions: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the torso before initiating the pull.
Drive your elbows down and back by contracting your biceps and lats, pulling your chest toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or the bar touches your upper chest, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the working muscles (biceps and upper back).
Lower yourself slowly and with control back to the full hang, extending the arms fully while maintaining shoulder blade stability.
Breathe out during the ascent and inhale during the controlled descent.
If you cannot complete a full chin-up, use a band-assisted variation, a box/step for negative-only reps, or an assisted chin-up machine to reduce load while maintaining the same body position.
exercises.description: A vertical pulling exercise performed from a hanging position with a supinated (underhand) grip that primarily loads the biceps while also working the lats and upper back. Pull your chin over the bar and lower under control to build upper-body pulling strength.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and chest pad (if present) so your feet are flat on the platform and the handles are within comfortable reach with your arms extended. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat and chest pad (if present) so your feet are flat on the platform and the handles are within comfortable reach with your arms extended.
Select an appropriate weight on the lever stack.
Sit upright with your chest against the pad (or seated tall if no pad), knees slightly bent, feet stable, and maintain a neutral spine and head alignment.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a firm, neutral grip; start with your arms fully extended and shoulders relaxed.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows backward and slightly down, retracting your shoulder blades to bring the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Keep elbows close to your sides, avoid excessive torso lean or shrugging the shoulders, and breathe out as you pull.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze your lats and upper back.
Slowly extend your arms to return the handles to the start position under control, allowing the shoulder blades to protract slightly but maintain tension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining neutral spine and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: Seated lever rows are a horizontal pulling exercise performed on a lever machine that primarily targets the lats while also engaging the middle and upper back and arm muscles. The movement involves pulling handles toward the torso with a scapular retraction and controlled return.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight, reach forward and grasp the handle with a neutral or double-handed grip, arms extended and spine straight (no rounded back). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the bench or seat facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Select an appropriate weight, reach forward and grasp the handle with a neutral or double-handed grip, arms extended and spine straight (no rounded back).
Engage your core, maintain an upright torso (straight back), and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together (scapular retraction).
Drive your elbows straight back toward your torso while keeping them close to your sides until the handle touches your abdomen or lower chest.
Pause and squeeze your lats/upper back at the end range for 1–2 seconds while avoiding excessive torso lean or trunk rotation.
Slowly extend your arms and allow the cable to return to the start position under control, keeping a straight back throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the pull and breathing in on the return.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Barbell Paused Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
Grip the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip (or mixed grip if needed for heavy loads) and brace your core.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together and driving your elbows back, bringing the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Hold and squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1–2 seconds (the paused portion) while keeping the torso stable and avoiding excess torso rotation.
Lower the bar under control until arms are extended and the bar is near knee level or a natural hang, maintaining a neutral spine throughout.
Repeat for desired reps while breathing out during the pull and inhaling as you lower; avoid rounding the back or using excessive momentum.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row performed from a hip-hinged position with a brief pause at the top of the pull to maximize scapular retraction and time under tension. Emphasizes rowing the elbows back while keeping the torso stable and the spine neutral.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Wide grip Row
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the bench/seat with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the wide rowing bar with a wide, overhand grip.
instructions: Sit upright on the bench/seat with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the wide rowing bar with a wide, overhand grip.
Start with arms extended, torso upright and chest up, maintaining a neutral spine and slight forward lean from the hips.
Initiate the movement by pulling the shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction), then drive the elbows out and back to bring the bar toward the upper abdomen/lower chest.
Squeeze the upper back and hold the contracted position for 1 second, keeping the neck neutral and avoiding excessive torso lean or momentum.
Slowly return the bar to the start by extending the arms under control while maintaining an engaged core and neutral spine; inhale on the return, exhale during the pull.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movements smooth and avoiding jerking or using the lower back to complete the pull.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip rows train the upper back by pulling a wide handle toward the torso, emphasizing the mid-back and rear shoulder muscles while minimizing lower-back involvement. Use controlled scapular retraction and elbow drive to maximize upper-back engagement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Back Seated Row
exercises.notes: Set your spine in a neutral (straight) position with chest up and shoulders relaxed—avoid rounding the back or excessive leaning.
instructions: Sit on the bench with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the close/V-handle with both hands and sit tall.
Set your spine in a neutral (straight) position with chest up and shoulders relaxed—avoid rounding the back or excessive leaning.
Begin the repetition by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction) to initiate the movement.
Continue pulling the handle toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows straight back; keep elbows close to the body.
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the end range for 1 second while maintaining an upright seated position (do not hyperextend the lower back).
Return the handle slowly under control by extending the arms and allowing the scapulae to protract until arms are nearly straight.
Breathe out as you pull, inhale as you return; use a controlled tempo and avoid using torso momentum to complete the reps.
exercises.description: Seated cable row performed with a straight, neutral spine; pull the handle toward the torso using shoulder blade retraction to emphasize the lats and upper back. Maintain an upright torso and controlled tempo throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Cable High Row with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Set two cable pulleys to a high position and attach single-hand or D handles; choose a moderate weight.
instructions: Set two cable pulleys to a high position and attach single-hand or D handles; choose a moderate weight.

Position an incline bench or chest pad in front of the machine so you can sit facing the pulleys with your chest pressed firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor; adjust pad height so torso is supported and arms can reach the handles overhead.
Sit tall with chest against the pad, neutral neck, core braced and shoulders down away from ears; reach up and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
Begin with arms nearly extended overhead, scapulae slightly protracted; inhale to prepare.
Exhale and pull the handles down and back toward the upper chest by driving your elbows down and out, leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together at the end range.
Hold the contracted position briefly (1–2 seconds) while maintaining a supported chest and no torso rotation.
Slowly release the handles under control, allowing the arms to extend back to the start while keeping tension on the cables and scapulae engaged.
Repeat for desired reps; avoid using momentum, leaning away from the pad, or shrugging the shoulders upward.
exercises.description: Seated chest-supported cable high row performed from dual high pulleys; you pull the handles down and back toward the upper chest while the chest rests against the pad to isolate the upper back. Emphasizes scapular retraction and posterior shoulder activation with reduced lower-back involvement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Fly
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push your butt back and keep a slight bend in the knees until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor; maintain a neutral spine and neck (bent-over position).
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip.
Hinge at the hips, push your butt back and keep a slight bend in the knees until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor; maintain a neutral spine and neck (bent-over position).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a small bend in the elbows; keep the shoulder blades relaxed but ready to retract.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc (horizontal abduction) leading with the elbows until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and at shoulder height.
Squeeze the rear delts and pinch the shoulder blades together at the top for a brief pause.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with control, avoiding momentum or torso swing.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping movement controlled and the torso stable throughout.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell fly that isolates the posterior (rear) deltoids by horizontally abducting the arms from a hinged position. Performed with a hip hinge and slight elbow bend, the movement emphasizes the rear delts while engaging the upper back and traps to stabilize and retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Clean and Press
exercises.notes: Step 1: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a full overhand or mixed grip and keep the chest up and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Step 1: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a full overhand or mixed grip and keep the chest up and core braced (standing position).
Step 2: Initiate the lift with a hip hinge by bending at the hips and knees keeping the spine neutral and shoulders slightly in front of the bar; maintain a tight core and engaged lats.

Step 3: Drive through the heels and extend the hips, knees and ankles explosively (triple extension) to accelerate the bar upward while shrugging the shoulders and pulling the elbows high and out (clean pull).

Step 4: As the bar reaches peak upward momentum, quickly drop under the bar into a partial front squat and rotate the elbows forward to receive the bar on the front of the shoulders in a front-rack position (clean, front-rack).

Step 5: Stand tall from the front-rack by extending the hips and knees to full standing, reset your core and grip, then press the bar overhead by extending the elbows and driving the head slightly forward until arms are fully locked out (press).
Step 6: Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position, then carefully return the bar to the floor with a hip hinge and bend the knees to reset for the next repetition.

Step 7: Maintain braced abs and a neutral spine throughout, breathe consistently (exhale during the press), and use controlled tempo—perform the clean explosively but receive and press with stability and control.
exercises.description: A compound Olympic-style lift combining a barbell clean (lifting the bar to a front-rack position) followed immediately by a press overhead, training the shoulders along with legs, hips and upper back. It develops explosive power, pressing strength, and full-body coordination.

exercises.name: Barbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (bent-over position), keeping a neutral spine and neck; let the bar hang straight down from your shoulders.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and a slight bend in the knees, holding a barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip about shoulder-width apart.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (bent-over position), keeping a neutral spine and neck; let the bar hang straight down from your shoulders.
Set your shoulder blades by lightly retracting them and maintain a slight bend in the elbows throughout the movement.
Exhale and lift the bar by driving your elbows out to the sides and squeezing the rear delts and upper back; lead the movement with the elbows so the hands travel outward and slightly back.
Raise until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (or in line with the shoulders), pause briefly while maintaining tightness in the delts and traps.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar back to the starting hanging position under control, avoiding momentum or excessive torso movement.
Use a lighter weight if you find yourself swinging or using lower-back extension; keep the motion strict and prioritize the posterior delts.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining a neutral spine, steady breathing (exhale on the lift, inhale on the lowering), and firm grip throughout.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged rear delt raise using a barbell to isolate the posterior deltoids by pulling the elbows out to the sides while keeping the torso bent over. Performed with a controlled, horizontal abduction motion to target the rear delts and upper back while the forearms maintain grip.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
